> 1. **Sign error in formula** (most common, 2 marks lost): The formula is
>    $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_{n-1} - x_n)}{f(x_{n-1}) - f(x_n)}$
>    Some students forget the **negative sign** in the numerator — this gives the wrong iteration direction.
>
> 2. **Swapped $x_{n-1}$ and $x_n$** (1 mark lost): Always place the **newer** value as $x_n$ and the **older** value as $x_{n-1}$.
>
> 3. **Skipping intermediate values in the iteration table** (1–2 marks lost): KTU valuation expects you to show **at least 4–5 rows** of the iteration table with $x_{n-1}$, $x_n$, $f(x_n)$, $x_{n+1}$, and the error $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to mention order of convergence** (2 marks lost): Even in a numerical problem, writing "Order = 1.618 (superlinear)" earns 1–2 bonus marks.
>
> 5. **No convergence check / no stopping criterion mentioned** (1 mark lost): Always end the solution with "$\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < 10^{-4}$" and checkmark.
>
> 6. **Geometric interpretation without diagram** (1–2 marks lost): KTU mandates a **neatly labeled sketch** for the geometric interpretation question. Always draw the curve, the secant line, the two points, and the x-intercept.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Secant Method** is an **open, derivative-free** root-finding algorithm needing **two** initial guesses $x_0, x_1$.
- ✅ **Main formula:** $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_{n-1} - x_n)}{f(x_{n-1}) - f(x_n)}$
- ✅ **Alternate form:** $x_{n+1} = \dfrac{x_{n-1} f(x_n) - x_n f(x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$
- ✅ **Order of convergence:** $\varphi = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ (the **Golden Ratio**) — *superlinear*.
- ✅ **Error relation:** $e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n \cdot e_{n-1}$
- ✅ **Connection to Newton-Raphson:** $f'(x_n)$ is replaced by the secant slope $\dfrac{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}{x_n - x_{n-1}}$.
- ✅ **Function evaluations per iteration:** 1 (vs. 2 for Newton-Raphson).
- ✅ **Convergence requirements:** $f$ continuous, differentiable near root, $f(x_0) \neq f(x_1)$, $f'(x) \neq 0$ at root.
- ✅ **Divergence risk:** Possible if $f(x_0) = f(x_1)$ (horizontal secant) or if guesses are far from root.
- ✅ **Stopping criterion:** $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ **OR** $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$.
- ✅ **Comparison:** Bisection (linear, 1) < **Secant (superlinear, 1.618)** < Newton-Raphson (quadratic, 2).
- ✅ **Advantages:** No derivative needed, fast, easy to code, robust for many problems.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** No guaranteed convergence, needs two initial guesses, may fail near local extrema.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** Power-flow equations, structural analysis, CFD solvers, Quasi-Newton optimization.
- ✅ **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the full iteration table — at least 4–5 rows — to claim full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: False Position Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** False Position Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — FALSE POSITION METHOD (REGULA FALSI)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FALSE POSITION METHOD (REGULA FALSI METHOD)**
> A **closed (bracketing) root-finding algorithm** used in numerical analysis to find a real root of a continuous nonlinear equation $f(x) = 0$ lying within an interval $[x_0, x_1]$, where $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$. The method estimates the root by **linear interpolation** (drawing a straight chord) between the two bracketing points $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$, and replacing the endpoint whose function value has the **same sign** as the new estimate. The iteration continues until $|f(x)|$ or the interval width drops below a predefined tolerance $\varepsilon$.

The phrase **"Regula Falsi"** is Latin, meaning **"Rule of False Position"** (or "false rule"), historically attributed to ancient Egyptian and Babylonian mathematicians.

### 1.2 Intuition — Real World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Treasure Hunt on a Mountain"**
> Imagine you are standing on one side of a mountain (elevation = $-1$ m) and your friend is on the other side (elevation = $+16$ m). You know that somewhere between you, the ground crosses sea level ($0$ m). Instead of walking to the exact midpoint (bisection), you **draw a straight line** between your two positions and **find where that imaginary line crosses zero**. That crossing point is your next guess. This guessed point is closer to the real root than the midpoint, because it respects the *slope* of the terrain. The false position method does exactly this: it uses the *shape* of the curve (via $f$ values at the endpoints) to make a smarter, linearly interpolated guess.

### 1.3 Key Parameters & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $x_0, x_1$ | Initial bracketing interval endpoints | — |
| $f(x_0), f(x_1)$ | Function values at endpoints | Sign must be opposite |
| $x_2$ | New approximation (false root) | Computed each iteration |
| $\varepsilon$ | **Stopping tolerance** | Typically $10^{-5}$ or $10^{-6}$ |
| $N_{max}$ | **Maximum iterations** | Usually 50–100 |
| $e_a$ | Approximate relative error | $\vert e_a \vert = \vert (x_{new} - x_{old}) / x_{new} \vert \times 100$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – KTU 2024):** The student must be able to *apply* the False Position Method to find a root of a non-linear equation and *implement* the same in **Python** using conditional loops and function evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step by Step

1. **Identify the bracket:** Choose $x_0$ and $x_1$ such that $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$. By the **Intermediate Value Theorem**, at least one root lies in $[x_0, x_1]$.
2. **Compute the new estimate $x_2$** using the linear interpolation formula (formula sheet below).
3. **Evaluate** $f(x_2)$.
4. **Sub-decide the bracket:**
   - If $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_2) < 0$ → root lies in $[x_0, x_2]$. Set $x_1 \leftarrow x_2$.
   - Else → root lies in $[x_2, x_1]$. Set $x_0 \leftarrow x_2$.
5. **Check convergence:** Stop if any of these conditions hold:
   - $\vert f(x_2) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (function value criterion)
   - $\vert x_2^{(new)} - x_2^{(old)} \vert \leq \varepsilon$ (interval width criterion)
   - Iteration count reaches $N_{max}$.
6. **Repeat** from step 2 with the updated bracket.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formula

Because $f(x)$ is continuous, we *approximate* the curve between $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$ as a straight line. The line connecting them in point-slope form is:

$$
\frac{y - f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

Setting $y = 0$ (i.e., finding where the chord crosses the $x$-axis) and solving for $x$ gives the false root $x_2$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Engineering Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $x_2 = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)\,(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$ | **Master interpolation formula** for False Position | Solving implicit equations in circuit analysis, beam design |
| 2 | $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$ | Bracket condition (root exists by IVT) | Initial guess validation |
| 3 | $e_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_2^{(k)} - x_2^{(k-1)}}{x_2^{(k)}} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | Approximate relative error | Convergence tracking |
| 4 | Order of convergence $= 1$ (linear) | Slower than Newton–Raphson; faster than bisection | Algorithm selection |
| 5 | $\dfrac{f(x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)} \in (-1, 0)$ if signs are opposite | Sanity check: $x_2$ must lie strictly inside $[x_0, x_1]$ | Numerical stability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Determining the depth of a foundation where bearing capacity curve crosses the load curve.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Solving for the operating point (Q-point) of a non-linear transistor equation $I_C = f(V_{BE})$.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Finding the equilibrium displacement in a non-linear spring system $F = kx + \alpha x^3$.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Solving implicit equations in thermodynamic property relations (e.g., Redlich–Kwong EOS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Snapshot:** Unlike **Newton–Raphson** (open method, quadratic convergence but needs $f'(x)$), the False Position Method is a *closed* method — it is **guaranteed to converge** provided the initial bracket is valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the False Position Formula

**Starting Point:** Two known points on the curve $y = f(x)$ are $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$.

**Step 1 — Equation of the chord (line) joining the two points** (two-point form):

$$
\frac{y - f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $y = 0$ to find where the chord crosses the $x$-axis** (this $x$ is our false root, call it $x_2$):

$$
\frac{0 - f(x_0)}{x_2 - x_0} = \frac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$
-f(x_0) \cdot (x_1 - x_0) = (x_2 - x_0) \cdot \big(f(x_1) - f(x_0)\big)
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $(x_2 - x_0)$:**

$$
x_2 - x_0 = \frac{-f(x_0) \cdot (x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_2$:**

$$
x_2 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0) \cdot (x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}
$$

This is the **master formula** of the False Position Method. ∎

---

## 3.2 Fully Worked Example (KTU Standard — 7 Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$ lying between $x_0 = 2$ and $x_1 = 3$ using the False Position Method. Perform **4 iterations** and state the approximate relative error after each step.

**Initial Evaluation:**

$$
f(2) = 2^3 - 2(2) - 5 = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1
$$

$$
f(3) = 3^3 - 2(3) - 5 = 27 - 6 - 5 = 16
$$

Since $f(2) \cdot f(3) = (-1)(16) = -16 < 0$, a root exists in $[2, 3]$. ✓

---

**Iteration 1:**

$$
x_2^{(1)} = 2 - \frac{(-1) \cdot (3 - 2)}{16 - (-1)} = 2 - \frac{(-1)(1)}{17} = 2 + \frac{1}{17} = 2.058823
$$

$$
f(x_2^{(1)}) = (2.058823)^3 - 2(2.058823) - 5
$$

$$
= 8.725832 - 4.117647 - 5 = -0.391815
$$

Since $f(2) \cdot f(x_2^{(1)}) = (-1)(-0.391815) > 0$ → root lies in $[2.058823, 3]$. Replace $x_0 \leftarrow 2.058823$.

---

**Iteration 2:**

$$
x_2^{(2)} = 2.058823 - \frac{(-0.391815) \cdot (3 - 2.058823)}{16 - (-0.391815)}
$$

$$
= 2.058823 - \frac{(-0.391815)(0.941177)}{16.391815}
$$

$$
= 2.058823 + \frac{0.368730}{16.391815} = 2.058823 + 0.022496 = 2.081319
$$

$$
f(2.081319) = (2.081319)^3 - 2(2.081319) - 5 = 9.014816 - 4.162638 - 5 = -0.147822
$$

Approximate relative error:

$$
e_a = \left\vert \frac{2.081319 - 2.058823}{2.081319} \right\vert \times 100\% = 1.0809\%
$$

Replace $x_0 \leftarrow 2.081319$.

---

**Iteration 3:**

$$
x_2^{(3)} = 2.081319 - \frac{(-0.147822) \cdot (3 - 2.081319)}{16 - (-0.147822)}
$$

$$
= 2.081319 - \frac{(-0.147822)(0.918681)}{16.147822}
$$

$$
= 2.081319 + \frac{0.135804}{16.147822} = 2.081319 + 0.008410 = 2.089729
$$

$$
f(2.089729) = (2.089729)^3 - 2(2.089729) - 5 = 9.125124 - 4.179458 - 5 = -0.054334
$$

$$
e_a = \left\vert \frac{2.089729 - 2.081319}{2.089729} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.4026\%
$$

---

**Iteration 4:**

$$
x_2^{(4)} = 2.089729 - \frac{(-0.054334)(3 - 2.089729)}{16 - (-0.054334)}
$$

$$
= 2.089729 + \frac{(0.054334)(0.910271)}{16.054334}
$$

$$
= 2.089729 + \frac{0.049460}{16.054334} = 2.089729 + 0.003081 = 2.092810
$$

$$
f(2.092810) = (2.092810)^3 - 2(2.092810) - 5 = 9.167816 - 4.185620 - 5 = -0.017804
$$

$$
e_a = \left\vert \frac{2.092810 - 2.089729}{2.092810} \right\vert \times 100\% = 0.1472\%
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Iter $k$ | $x_0$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f(x_2)$ | $e_a$ (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 2.000000 | 3.000000 | 2.058823 | $-0.391815$ | — |
| 2 | 2.058823 | 3.000000 | 2.081319 | $-0.147822$ | 1.0809 |
| 3 | 2.081319 | 3.000000 | 2.089729 | $-0.054334$ | 0.4026 |
| 4 | 2.089729 | 3.000000 | 2.092810 | $-0.017804$ | 0.1472 |

> [!NOTE]
> The true root of $x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ is $x \approx 2.094551$. After 4 iterations, our estimate $\approx 2.0928$ is within $0.0017$ of the true value. The method shows **monotonic convergence**.

---

## 3.4 Python Implementation (Reference Algorithm — Course-Aligned)

```python
import math

def f(x):
    return x**3 - 2*x - 5

def false_position(x0, x1, tol=1e-6, Nmax=50):
    if f(x0) * f(x1) >= 0:
        raise ValueError("f(x0) and f(x1) must have opposite signs.")
    print(f"{'k':>3} {'x0':>12} {'x1':>12} {'x2':>12} {'f(x2)':>14} {'ea(%)':>10}")
    x2_old = x0
    for k in range(1, Nmax + 1):
        x2 = x0 - f(x0) * (x1 - x0) / (f(x1) - f(x0))
        ea = abs((x2 - x2_old) / x2) * 100 if k > 1 else float('nan')
        print(f"{k:>3} {x0:>12.6f} {x1:>12.6f} {x2:>12.6f} {f(x2):>14.6f} {ea:>10.4f}")
        if abs(f(x2)) < tol:
            break
        if f(x0) * f(x2) < 0:
            x1 = x2
        else:
            x0 = x2
        x2_old = x2
    return x2

root = false_position(2, 3)
print(f"Approximate root = {root:.6f}")
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Input x0, x1, epsilon, Nmax]) --> chkBracket{f x0 times f x1 < 0 ?}
    chkBracket -- NO --> errBox[RAISE ERROR: No bracket. Change initial guesses.] --> stopZ([STOP])
    chkBracket -- YES --> initA[Set k = 1, x2_old = x0]
    initA --> loopChk{k less than or equal to Nmax ?}
    loopChk -- NO --> stopY([STOP: Max iterations reached])
    loopChk -- YES --> calcX2[Compute x2 = x0 - f x0 times x1 minus x0 divided by f x1 - f x0]
    calcX2 --> evalFx2[Evaluate f x2]
    evalFx2 --> printRow[Print k, x0, x1, x2, f x2, ea]
    printRow --> convChk{abs f x2 less than epsilon OR ea less than epsilon ?}
    convChk -- YES --> stopX([STOP: Converged. Return x2 as root])
    convChk -- NO --> signChk{f x0 times f x2 < 0 ?}
    signChk -- YES --> updX1[Set x1 = x2]
    signChk -- NO --> updX0[Set x0 = x2]
    updX1 --> incK[Set x2_old = x2, k = k + 1]
    updX0 --> incK
    incK --> loopChk
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture (Bracket Update Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT MODULE"]
        i1[Read x0, x1] --> i2[Read tolerance epsilon]
        i2 --> i3[Read max iterations Nmax]
    end

    subgraph CORE["ITERATIVE CORE"]
        c1[Function Evaluator f x] --> c2[False Position Formula]
        c2 --> c3[Bracket Decider]
        c3 --> c4[Convergence Checker]
        c4 --> c5[Iteration Counter]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT MODULE"]
        o1[Tabular Iteration Log] --> o2[Final Root Estimate]
        o2 --> o3[Error Plot Optional]
    end

    INPUT --> CORE
    CORE --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 Geometric Interpretation (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GEO["GEOMETRIC MEANING OF ONE ITERATION"]
        p1[Point A: x0, f x0 — say negative]:::negPt
        p2[Point B: x1, f x1 — say positive]:::posPt
        chord[Straight Line Chord joining A and B]:::chordLine
        root[False Root x2: where chord crosses x axis]:::rootPt
        actual[True Root x_true lies on curve below chord]:::actualPt
    end

    p1 --- chord
    p2 --- chord
    chord -.intersects x axis.-> root
    actual --- root

    classDef negPt fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#900
    classDef posPt fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#060
    classDef chordLine fill:#fffbe5,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px,color:#660
    classDef rootPt fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:3px,color:#036
    classDef actualPt fill:#f0e5ff,stroke:#606,stroke-width:2px,color:#303
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the False Position formula. Under what condition is the method guaranteed to converge?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The False Position (Regula Falsi) formula for finding a root of $f(x) = 0$ in $[x_0, x_1]$ is:

$$
x_2 = x_0 - \frac{f(x_0)\,(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}
$$

**Convergence condition:** The method is a *closed bracketing* method. It is **guaranteed to converge** provided:

1. $f(x)$ is **continuous** on $[x_0, x_1]$, and
2. The initial bracket is **valid**, i.e., $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) < 0$, ensuring at least one root exists in the interval by the Intermediate Value Theorem. *(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for condition and justification.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Bisection Method and the False Position Method in terms of convergence rate and formula used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bisection Method | False Position Method |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Formula** | $x_2 = \dfrac{x_0 + x_1}{2}$ | $x_2 = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$ |
| **Convergence rate** | Linear, with guaranteed error halving each step | Linear (order 1) but generally **faster** in practice |
| **Use of function shape** | Ignores $f$ values (uses midpoint) | Uses function values at endpoints (linear interpolation) |
| **Number of $f$ evaluations** | 1 per iteration | 1 per iteration (same as bisection) |

*(1 mark per key point; minimum 3 distinct comparisons expected.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting with the equation of a straight line joining two points, **derive the False Position formula**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the False Position Method, find the root of $f(x) = x^3 + x^2 - 3x - 3$ in the interval $[1, 2]$ correct to **four decimal places**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q A (a)

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps to write:

1. Two-point form: $\dfrac{y - f(x_0)}{x - x_0} = \dfrac{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}{x_1 - x_0}$ *(1 mark)*
2. Set $y = 0$ to find $x$-intercept *(1 mark)*
3. Cross-multiply: $-f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0) = (x_2 - x_0)[f(x_1) - f(x_0)]$ *(1 mark)*
4. Rearrange to isolate $x_2 - x_0$ *(1 mark)*
5. Final formula: $x_2 = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$ *(3 marks for stating with explanation)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Initial check:**

$$
f(1) = 1 + 1 - 3 - 3 = -4
$$

$$
f(2) = 8 + 4 - 6 - 3 = 3
$$

Since $f(1) \cdot f(2) = -12 < 0$, root lies in $[1, 2]$. ✓

**Iteration 1:**

$$
x_2 = 1 - \frac{(-4)(2 - 1)}{3 - (-4)} = 1 + \frac{4}{7} = 1.571429
$$

$$
f(1.571429) = (1.571429)^3 + (1.571429)^2 - 3(1.571429) - 3
$$

$$
= 3.881184 + 2.469388 - 4.714286 - 3 = -1.363714
$$

New bracket: $[1.571429, 2]$. $e_a = \text{N/A}$.

**Iteration 2:**

$$
x_2 = 1.571429 - \frac{(-1.363714)(2 - 1.571429)}{3 - (-1.363714)}
$$

$$
= 1.571429 + \frac{(1.363714)(0.428571)}{4.363714} = 1.571429 + 0.133929 = 1.705358
$$

$$
f(1.705358) = 4.960 + 2.908 - 5.116 - 3 = -0.247718
$$

$$
e_a = \left\vert \frac{1.705358 - 1.571429}{1.705358} \right\vert \times 100\% = 7.8523\%
$$

New bracket: $[1.705358, 2]$.

**Iteration 3:**

$$
x_2 = 1.705358 - \frac{(-0.247718)(0.294642)}{3.247718} = 1.705358 + 0.022471 = 1.727829
$$

$$
f(1.727829) \approx 5.1576 + 2.9854 - 5.1835 - 3 = -0.040549
$$

$$
e_a = \left\vert \frac{1.727829 - 1.705358}{1.727829} \right\vert \times 100\% = 1.3004\%
$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$
x_2 = 1.727829 - \frac{(-0.040549)(0.272171)}{3.040549} = 1.727829 + 0.003629 = 1.731458
$$

$$
f(1.731458) \approx 5.1912 + 2.9980 - 5.1944 - 3 = -0.005184
$$

$$
e_a \approx 0.21\%
$$

**Iteration 5:**

$$
x_2 \approx 1.732050 \quad (f(x_2) \approx -0.000015)
$$

$$
e_a \approx 0.0034\%
$$

**Result:** The root, correct to four decimal places, is $\boxed{x \approx 1.7321}$ *(7 marks — distributed across correct formula use, evaluation, error calculation, and final answer.)*

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **geometric interpretation** of the False Position Method. How does it differ from Newton–Raphson geometrically?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find a root of $f(x) = \cos(x) - x\,e^{x}$ in $[0, 1]$ using the False Position Method to an accuracy of $10^{-4}$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Geometric Interpretation (False Position):**

The method replaces the curve $y = f(x)$ between two bracketing points by a **straight chord**. The $x$-intercept of this chord becomes the next approximation. Geometrically:

- In False Position → we use a **chord (secant line through two points)** to approximate the curve.
- In Newton–Raphson → we use the **tangent line at the current point** (requires $f'(x)$).

Therefore, Newton–Raphson needs only **one initial guess** and **first derivative computation**, while False Position needs **two initial guesses** (bracket) but no derivative. Newton–Raphson has **quadratic convergence**, whereas False Position has **linear convergence**. False Position is **safer (always converges in a bracket)**; Newton–Raphson may **diverge** for poor initial guesses. *(7 marks: 3 marks for False Position geometry, 2 marks for Newton–Raphson geometry, 2 marks for comparison.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Initial evaluation** (using $f(x) = \cos(x) - x e^{x}$):

$$
f(0) = \cos(0) - 0 = 1
$$

$$
f(1) = \cos(1) - e^1 = 0.5403 - 2.7183 = -2.1780
$$

Since $f(0) \cdot f(1) < 0$, root lies in $[0, 1]$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$
x_2 = 0 - \frac{1 \cdot (1 - 0)}{-2.1780 - 1} = 0 - \frac{1}{-3.1780} = 0.3147
$$

$$
f(0.3147) = \cos(0.3147) - 0.3147 \cdot e^{0.3147} = 0.9511 - 0.4403 = 0.5108
$$

**Iteration 2:** $x_2 = 0.3147 - \dfrac{0.5108 \cdot (1 - 0.3147)}{-2.1780 - 0.5108} = 0.3147 + 0.1632 = 0.4779$

$f(0.4779) = \cos(0.4779) - 0.4779 \cdot e^{0.4779} = 0.8888 - 0.7723 = 0.1165$

**Iteration 3:** $x_2 = 0.4779 - \dfrac{0.1165 \cdot 0.5221}{-2.2945} = 0.4779 + 0.0265 = 0.5044$

$f(0.5044) = \cos(0.5044) - 0.5044 \cdot e^{0.5044} \approx 0.8741 - 0.8335 = 0.0406$

**Iteration 4:** $x_2 = 0.5044 + \dfrac{0.0406 \cdot 0.4956}{2.2186} = 0.5044 + 0.00907 = 0.5135$

$e_a = \left\vert \dfrac{0.5135 - 0.5044}{0.5135} \right\vert \times 100\% = 1.77\%$ — Continue.

**Iteration 5:** $x_2 \approx 0.5176$, $e_a \approx 0.79\%$

**Iteration 6:** $x_2 \approx 0.5191$, $e_a \approx 0.29\%$

**Iteration 7:** $x_2 \approx 0.5195$, $e_a \approx 0.08\% < 0.01\%$

**Result:** The root is $\boxed{x \approx 0.5195}$ to accuracy $10^{-4}$. *(7 marks: 1 mark bracket check, 4 marks iterations, 1 mark error analysis, 1 mark final answer.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Formula:** Students often write $x_2 = x_0 + \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$ (missing the minus sign). Always remember the formula has a **minus** sign — it comes from setting $y = 0$, not $y = f(x_0)$. **−1 to −2 marks deduction.**
>
> 2. **Wrong Bracket Replacement:** After computing $x_2$, students sometimes **replace the wrong endpoint**. Rule: if $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_2) < 0$, the root is in $[x_0, x_2]$, so **replace $x_1$** (NOT $x_0$). **−1 mark per such iteration.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting Sign of $f(x_2)$:** When computing $e_a$ or checking convergence, students sometimes report $|f(x_2)|$ without evaluating the sign correctly. KTU expects the **signed value** in the table.
>
> 4. **Not Verifying Initial Bracket:** If $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_1) > 0$, the method is **undefined** for that interval. Examiners often give an invalid bracket to test this. **−1 mark if skipped.**
>
> 5. **Stopping Too Early:** Stop only when **consecutive** $x_2$ values match to the required decimal places. Some students stop after one iteration — full marks lost.
>
> 6. **Confusing "Iterations" with "Function Evaluations":** Each iteration needs **1 new** $f(x_2)$ evaluation (the other two are reused). Misreporting this loses half-mark style deductions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **False Position Method** is a *closed* bracketing method — guaranteed to converge if the initial bracket is valid.
- Master formula: $x_2 = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)(x_1 - x_0)}{f(x_1) - f(x_0)}$
- Bracket replacement rule:
  - $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_2) < 0$ → replace **$x_1$** with $x_2$.
  - $f(x_0) \cdot f(x_2) > 0$ → replace **$x_0$** with $x_2$.
- **Convergence order:** Linear (order 1) — slower than Newton–Raphson but safer.
- **Convergence criterion:** Stop when $|f(x_2)| < \varepsilon$ **OR** $|x_2^{(k)} - x_2^{(k-1)}| < \varepsilon$.
- **Relative error formula:** $e_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_2^{(new)} - x_2^{(old)}}{x_2^{(new)}} \right\vert \times 100\%$
- **Latin origin:** "Regula Falsi" = "Rule of False Position".
- **Drawback:** One endpoint can get "stuck" if $f$ is highly concave near the root — the method may converge very slowly from one side. (Modified Regula Falsi / Illinois algorithm addresses this.)
- **KTU 2024 Mapping:** Aligned with **CO1** — *Apply numerical methods to solve nonlinear equations using Python programming constructs.*
- **Python hint:** Use a `while` or `for` loop with a counter, conditional `if` for bracket update, and `math.fabs()` for absolute value comparison.
- **Exam Tip:** Always present a **tabular iteration log** with columns: $k$, $x_0$, $x_1$, $x_2$, $f(x_2)$, $e_a$. This is the **single most important** habit that earns full marks in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed-Point Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fixed-Point Iteration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixed-Point Iteration (FPI)** is an *open-bracket* numerical root-finding scheme used to obtain a real root $\alpha$ of a nonlinear equation $f(x)=0$ by re-engineering the equation into the equivalent form $x = g(x)$ and repeatedly substituting successive approximations into the recurrence $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$.

A point $\alpha$ such that $\alpha = g(\alpha)$ is called a **fixed point** of $g(x)$. Finding a zero of $f(x)$ is therefore reduced to finding a fixed point of a suitably constructed *iteration function* $g(x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under *Module 1 — Solutions of Equations in One Variable* and is one of the three foundational open methods (the other two being **Newton–Raphson** and **Secant**). It is frequently tested as a 3-mark conceptual question and as a 14-mark computational sub-part.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Thermostat on a Hot Day

Imagine a room whose temperature $T$ is governed by an outside heat source. The thermostat's logic can be modelled as:

$$T_{n+1} = g(T_n)$$

where $g$ "predicts" the next temperature from the current one. The thermostat keeps cycling until the prediction no longer changes — i.e. it reaches a **fixed point** $T^\star$ where $T^\star = g(T^\star)$.

In root-finding terms, $f(x)=0$ asks: *"At what input $x$ does the function $f$ touch zero?"* Fixed-point iteration asks: *"At what input $x$ does the transformed function $g$ return the input back?"* — two sides of the same coin.

### 1.3 Geometric Picture

The fixed point is the intersection of the curve $y = g(x)$ and the line $y = x$. As iterations proceed, successive values $x_n$ are "pulled" toward this intersection point — provided the slope of $g$ at the fixed point is not too steep.

### 1.4 Why a Single $f(x)=0$ Can Be Rebuilt As Multiple $g(x)=x$

> [!TIP]
> A given equation $f(x)=0$ admits **infinitely many** valid choices of $g(x)$. For example, $x^2 - 4 = 0$ may be written as:
> - $g_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 4}{2x}$ (Aitken / Newton-like, **quadratic**),
> - $g_2(x) = \dfrac{x + 4}{x}$ (linear, oscillates),
> - $g_3(x) = 4/x$ (linear, **diverges** at $x=2$).
>
> Choosing the "best" $g$ is the **engineering art** behind the method.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of the Iteration Function

To obtain a fixed-point scheme from $f(x)=0$ we apply an **algebraic rearrangement** of the form

$$x = g(x) \equiv x - \phi(x)\,f(x)$$

where $\phi(x) \neq 0$ is an *auxiliary* function chosen by the analyst. Different $\phi(x)$ produce different $g(x)$ — and dramatically different convergence behaviour.

| $\phi(x)$ Choice | Resulting $g(x)$ | Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\phi(x) = \dfrac{1}{f'(x)}$ | $g(x) = x - \dfrac{f(x)}{f'(x)}$ | Newton–Raphson |
| $\phi(x) = c$ (constant) | $g(x) = x - c\,f(x)$ | Linear-FPI |
| $\phi(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ | $g(x) = x - \dfrac{f(x)}{x}$ | Modified forms |

### 2.2 Algorithm Logic — Step by Step

1. **Re-engineer** $f(x)=0$ into $x=g(x)$ ensuring $g$ is continuous and differentiable in a neighbourhood of the root.
2. **Pick** an initial approximation $x_0$.
3. **Iterate** $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ for $n=0,1,2,\dots$
4. **Terminate** when either
$$\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon \quad \text{(absolute successive-error test)} \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon \quad \text{(relative test)}$$
5. **Report** $x_{n+1}$ as the root $\alpha$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the single most important reference card for this topic. Memorise each row.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Symbol/Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Iteration formula | $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ | Recurrence |
| 2 | Fixed point definition | $\alpha = g(\alpha)$ | Equivalent to $f(\alpha)=0$ |
| 3 | **Sufficient convergence condition** | $\vert g'(x) \vert < 1$ in an interval $I$ containing $\alpha$ | *Contraction mapping* requirement |
| 4 | **Necessary condition** | $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$ at the root | Local criterion |
| 5 | Asymptotic error relation | $e_{n+1} \approx g'(\alpha)\,e_n$ | Linear convergence |
| 6 | Asymptotic error constant | $\lambda = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ | Smaller $\lambda$ → faster |
| 7 | Order of convergence | $p = 1$ (linear) if $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$ | Higher if $g'(\alpha)=0$ |
| 8 | Termination — absolute | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ | Standard |
| 9 | Termination — relative | $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$ | Avoids scale issues |
| 10 | Divergence criterion | $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert > 1$ | Series explodes |
| 11 | Oscillation / 2-cycle | $g'(\alpha) \approx -1$ | Sign-alternating sequence |
| 12 | Spectral radius | $\rho = \max \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ in $I$ | $\rho < 1 \Leftrightarrow$ convergence |

### 2.4 The Theoretical "Why" — Three Foundational Theorems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask *"State the conditions for convergence"* (3 marks). The three theorems below give the *complete* answer.

**Theorem A — Existence & Uniqueness (Fixed-Point Theorem).**
If $g(x)$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and $g(x) \in [a,b]$ for all $x \in [a,b]$, then $g$ has at least one fixed point in $[a,b]$. If, in addition, $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$ on $[a,b]$, then the fixed point is **unique**.

**Theorem B — Convergence Rate.**
Let $e_n = x_n - \alpha$ and assume $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$. Then
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{e_{n+1}}{e_n} = g'(\alpha)$$
which establishes **linear convergence** with rate constant $\lambda = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$.

**Theorem C — Sufficient Convergence Test (KTU Favourite).**
If $g$ satisfies $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$ for all $x \in [a,b]$ and $g(x) \in [a,b]$, then FPI starting from any $x_0 \in [a,b]$ converges to the unique fixed point $\alpha$ with the error bound
$$\vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1-k}\,\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$$

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Fixed-point schemes are the engine behind **successive-approximation ADCs**, **iterative decoder convergence in 5G LDPC codes**, **equilibrium analysis in structural mechanics**, **steady-state solvers in CFD** (SIMPLE algorithm), and **PID loop auto-tuning** in control engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Asymptotic Error Recurrence

Let $\alpha$ be the exact root, i.e. $\alpha = g(\alpha)$, and let the iterate satisfy $x_n = \alpha + e_n$ where $e_n$ is the absolute error. We expand $g(x_n)$ in a Taylor series about the fixed point $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{n+1} &= g(x_n) = g(\alpha + e_n) \\
        &= g(\alpha) + e_n\,g'(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{\,2}}{2!}\,g''(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{\,3}}{3!}\,g'''(\alpha) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the fixed-point condition $g(\alpha) = \alpha$, the relation $x_{n+1} = \alpha + e_{n+1}$ yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha + e_{n+1} &= \alpha + e_n\,g'(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{\,2}}{2!}\,g''(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{\,3}}{3!}\,g'''(\alpha) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancelling $\alpha$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{n+1} &= e_n\,g'(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{\,2}}{2!}\,g''(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{\,3}}{3!}\,g'''(\alpha) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic explained:** The left-hand side is the next-iteration error; the right-hand side expresses it as a polynomial in the current error, with derivatives of $g$ at the fixed point acting as coefficients. As $n \to \infty$, $e_n \to 0$, so the higher-order terms become negligible compared to the first. Hence, for *sufficiently small* $e_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{n+1} &\approx e_n\,g'(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that the **error contracts by a factor of $g'(\alpha)$** at every step — the analytical heart of linear convergence.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — The Golden Ratio

We solve the fixed-point problem $x = g(x) = 1 + \dfrac{1}{x}$. The true fixed point is $\alpha = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.6180339887$.

**Step 1 — Convergence check.**
$$g'(x) = -\dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$$
At the fixed point, $g'(\alpha) = -\dfrac{1}{\alpha^{2}} = -\dfrac{1}{2.618\ldots} \approx -0.3820$.
Since $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert = 0.382 < 1$, **the iteration will converge** with linear rate $\lambda = 0.382$.

**Step 2 — Iterate from $x_0 = 1.0$.**

| $n$ | $x_n$ (5 d.p.) | $e_n = \alpha - x_n$ | Ratio $\dfrac{e_{n+1}}{e_n}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 1.00000 | +0.61803 | — |
| 1 | 2.00000 | −0.38197 | −0.6180 |
| 2 | 1.50000 | +0.11803 | −0.3091 |
| 3 | 1.66667 | −0.04863 | −0.4119 |
| 4 | 1.60000 | +0.01803 | −0.3708 |
| 5 | 1.62500 | −0.00697 | −0.3865 |
| 6 | 1.61538 | +0.00265 | −0.3802 |
| 7 | 1.61905 | −0.00101 | −0.3811 |
| 8 | 1.61765 | +0.00039 | −0.3861 |
| 9 | 1.61818 | −0.00015 | −0.3846 |
| 10 | 1.61798 | +0.00006 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Column 4 settles towards $-0.382$, exactly the theoretical value $g'(\alpha) = -1/\alpha^2$. The sign alternation (oscillation) is consistent with $g'(\alpha)$ being negative.

**Step 3 — Verification of error bound.**
The error bound from Theorem C with $k = 0.382$ and $\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert = 1$ gives
$$\vert x_{10} - \alpha \vert \leq \dfrac{0.382^{10}}{1 - 0.382} \times 1 \approx 6.65 \times 10^{-5}$$
The actual error $\vert e_{10} \vert \approx 6 \times 10^{-5}$ lies **inside the bound** ✓.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Choice of $g(x)$ Affects Convergence

Consider $f(x) = x^2 - 4 = 0$, root $\alpha = 2$. Below are **three** valid rearrangements and their local behaviour.

| $g(x)$ | $g'(x)$ | $\vert g'(2) \vert$ | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $g_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 4}{2x}$ | $\dfrac{x^2 - 4}{2x^2}$ | $0$ | **Quadratic** (Newton–Raphson analogue) |
| $g_2(x) = \dfrac{x + 4}{x}$ | $-\dfrac{4}{x^2}$ | $1$ | **Marginal** — may oscillate / diverge |
| $g_3(x) = 4/x$ | $-\dfrac{4}{x^2}$ | $1$ | **Oscillates** in a 2-cycle |
| $g_4(x) = \dfrac{x}{2} + 1$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $0.5$ | **Linear** convergence to 1 (not a root — wrong!) |
| $g_5(x) = \sqrt{x+2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x+2}}$ | $0.25$ | **Linear** convergence to $2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1:** A valid $g(x)$ may have a fixed point that is *not* a root of $f(x)$. Always verify the fixed point by substituting back into $f$.

### 3.4 Three Derivations of the Convergence Speed

**Derivation 1 — Linear convergence factor.**
From $e_{n+1} \approx g'(\alpha)\,e_n$, by induction
$$e_n \approx [g'(\alpha)]^n\,e_0$$
Taking logarithms:
$$\ln \vert e_n \vert \approx n \ln \vert g'(\alpha) \vert + \ln \vert e_0 \vert$$
This is a straight line in $n$ — the signature of *linear* (first-order) convergence.

**Derivation 2 — Number of iterations to reach tolerance $\varepsilon$.**
Require $\vert e_n \vert < \varepsilon$:
$$[g'(\alpha)]^n\, \vert e_0 \vert < \varepsilon \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n > \dfrac{\ln(\varepsilon / \vert e_0 \vert)}{\ln \vert g'(\alpha) \vert}$$

**Derivation 3 — Contraction mapping guarantee.**
By the Mean Value Theorem, for any $x, y \in [a,b]$:
$$\vert g(x) - g(y) \vert = \vert g'(\xi) \vert\, \vert x - y \vert \leq k\,\vert x - y \vert, \quad k < 1$$
Therefore
$$\vert x_{n+1} - \alpha \vert = \vert g(x_n) - g(\alpha) \vert \leq k\,\vert x_n - \alpha \vert$$
which by induction gives geometric decay $\vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq k^n \vert x_0 - \alpha \vert$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FPI

The diagram below maps the **information flow** between the major sub-modules of a generic fixed-point iteration engine. Each block represents a conceptual sub-routine, not a physical circuit, but the control flow is rigorous.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[/Input f-of-x, g-of-x, x-naught, epsilon/]
    A1 --> A2[Pre-process: rearrange f to x = g of x]
    A2 --> A3[Verify contraction: vertical g-prime of x vertical less than 1 near alpha]
    A3 --> A4{Contraction satisfied?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[/Warn: divergence likely/] --> A6([Stop])
    A4 -- Yes --> A7[Initialise n = 0, x-current = x-naught]
    A7 --> A8[Compute x-next = g of x-current]
    A8 --> A9[Increment n to n+1]
    A9 --> A10[Compute error = vertical x-next minus x-current vertical]
    A10 --> A11{Error less than epsilon OR n greater than N-max?}
    A11 -- No --> A8
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[/Output root approx = x-next, iterations = n/]
    A12 --> A13([Stop])

    style A0 fill:#dff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A13 fill:#dff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#fdd,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#ffe,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A11 fill:#eef,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Cobweb (Staircase) Diagram — Conceptual Map

The classical *cobweb plot* visualises why $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$ guarantees convergence. Below is its functional equivalent as a state machine: each box represents a logical state of the iteration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[State: starting value x-naught] --> s2[Vertical jump to curve y = g of x]
    s2 --> s3[Horizontal jump to line y = x]
    s3 --> s4{Is vertical g-prime of alpha vertical less than 1?}
    s4 -- Yes --> s5[State: spiral inward to fixed point alpha]
    s4 -- No --> s6[State: spiral outward to infinity]
    s5 --> s7[State: convergence achieved]
    s6 --> s8[State: divergence]
    s7 --> s9([Outcome: x-n converges to alpha])
    s8 --> s10([Outcome: x-n diverges or oscillates])

    style s1 fill:#cfe,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style s5 fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style s6 fill:#fcc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style s9 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style s10 fill:#f99,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mapping of Engineering Sub-Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    e1[Engineering Domain] --> e2[Structural equilibrium solver]
    e1 --> e3[Control systems: PID steady state]
    e1 --> e4[Signal processing: fixed-point DSP]
    e1 --> e5[Telecom: iterative decoding]
    e2 --> f1[Iteration g derived from stiffness equation]
    e3 --> f2[Iteration g derived from error dynamics]
    e4 --> f3[Iteration g derived from filter response]
    e5 --> f4[Iteration g derived from code constraints]
    f1 --> r1[Apply FPI engine]
    f2 --> r1
    f3 --> r1
    f4 --> r1
    r1([Single algorithmic kernel: x-n+1 = g of x-n])

    style e1 fill:#eef,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style r1 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define the method of fixed-point iteration for finding a root of $f(x)=0$. What is meant by a "fixed point"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fixed-point iteration is an open method that finds a real root of $f(x)=0$ by rewriting the equation as $x = g(x)$ and generating the sequence $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ starting from an initial approximation $x_0$ *(1 mark)*. A point $\alpha$ such that $\alpha = g(\alpha)$ is called a **fixed point** of $g$ *(1 mark)*. If $g$ is continuous and $\vert g'(x) \vert < 1$ in an interval containing $\alpha$, the sequence converges to $\alpha$ for $x_0$ sufficiently close to $\alpha$ *(1 mark)*.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *State the necessary and sufficient conditions for the convergence of the fixed-point iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Necessary condition:** $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$ in a neighbourhood of the fixed point and the iteration function $g$ must be continuous and differentiable in the interval of interest *(1 mark)*. **Sufficient condition:** If $g$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, $g(x) \in [a,b]$ for all $x \in [a,b]$, and there exists $k < 1$ such that $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k$ on $[a,b]$ *(1 mark)*, then the iteration converges to a **unique** fixed point for any $x_0 \in [a,b]$ with error bound $\vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1-k}\,\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$ *(1 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Computational Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Rearrangement & Iteration Table**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Derive the asymptotic error formula $e_{n+1} = g'(\alpha)\,e_n$ for the fixed-point iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ using the iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ where $g(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{5 + 2x}{1}}$. Start with $x_0 = 2.0$ and continue until successive iterates agree to $4$ decimal places. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Let $\alpha$ be the exact fixed point, $x_n = \alpha + e_n$. Apply Taylor expansion of $g$ about $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{n+1} &= g(x_n) = g(\alpha + e_n) \\
        &= g(\alpha) + e_n\,g'(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{2}}{2!}\,g''(\alpha) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\alpha = g(\alpha)$ and $x_{n+1} = \alpha + e_{n+1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha + e_{n+1} &= \alpha + e_n\,g'(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{2}}{2!}\,g''(\alpha) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{n+1} &= e_n\,g'(\alpha) + \dfrac{e_n^{2}}{2!}\,g''(\alpha) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

For sufficiently small $e_n$ (as $n \to \infty$), higher-order terms vanish and:

$$
\boxed{\,e_{n+1} \approx g'(\alpha)\,e_n\,}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- The error contracts by a factor of $g'(\alpha)$ each step → **linear convergence** with rate $\lambda = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ *(1 mark)*.
- A *small* $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ yields *fast* convergence; $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert \geq 1$ causes divergence or oscillation *(1 mark)*.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Convergence test:** $g(x) = \sqrt{5 + 2x} \;\Rightarrow\; g'(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5 + 2x}}$.

Near the root, $x \approx 2.1$, so $\vert g'(2.1) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{9.2}} \approx 0.330 < 1$ ✓ **convergent** *(1 mark)*.

**Iteration table** (6 marks):

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $x_{n+1} = \sqrt{5 + 2x_n}$ | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 2.0000 | 3.0000 | 1.0000 |
| 1 | 3.0000 | 3.3166 | 0.3166 |
| 2 | 3.3166 | 3.4487 | 0.1321 |
| 3 | 3.4487 | 3.5060 | 0.0573 |
| 4 | 3.5060 | 3.5310 | 0.0250 |
| 5 | 3.5310 | 3.5419 | 0.0109 |
| 6 | 3.5419 | 3.5466 | 0.0047 |
| 7 | 3.5466 | 3.5486 | 0.0020 |
| 8 | 3.5486 | 3.5495 | 0.0009 |
| 9 | 3.5495 | 3.5499 | 0.0004 |
| 10 | 3.5499 | 3.5501 | 0.0002 |
| 11 | 3.5501 | 3.5501 | 0.0000 |

At $n=11$, successive iterates agree to **4 decimal places** ✓.
$$\boxed{\,\alpha \approx 3.5501\,}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Convergence Analysis & Alternative Forms**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternative]**
> **(a)** For the equation $f(x) = x^2 - 4 = 0$, three iteration functions are proposed:
> $g_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 4}{2x}$, $\;g_2(x) = \dfrac{4}{x}$, $\;g_3(x) = 1 + \dfrac{x}{2}$.
> Determine which iteration(s) will converge to the positive root $x = 2$ and justify your answer analytically. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from $x_0 = 3.5$, perform four iterations of the *convergent* scheme identified in (a) and estimate the asymptotic error constant from the iteration data. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

For each $g$, compute $g'(x)$ and evaluate $\vert g'(2) \vert$:

**For $g_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 4}{2x}$** *(2 marks)*:

$$
g_1'(x) = \dfrac{(2x)(2x) - (x^2 + 4)(2)}{(2x)^2} = \dfrac{4x^2 - 2x^2 - 8}{4x^2} = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{2x^2}
$$

At $x = 2$: $g_1'(2) = \dfrac{4 - 4}{8} = 0$. Since $\vert 0 \vert < 1$ → **converges (in fact, quadratically)**.

**For $g_2(x) = 4/x$** *(2 marks)*:

$$
g_2'(x) = -\dfrac{4}{x^2}
$$

At $x = 2$: $g_2'(2) = -1$. Since $\vert -1 \vert = 1$ → **does NOT converge** (2-cycle oscillation).

**For $g_3(x) = 1 + x/2$** *(2 marks)*:

$$
g_3'(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

At $x = 2$: $g_3'(2) = 0.5$. Although $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$ → **converges**, but to the fixed point $1 + 2/2 = 2$. ✓ (Note: $g_3$ does converge, but $g_3'(2) = 0.5$, not zero.)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** $g_1$ and $g_3$ both converge to $\alpha = 2$, but $g_1$ is **faster** (quadratic vs linear). $g_2$ **diverges** (oscillates).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Use $g_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 4}{2x}$ with $x_0 = 3.5$:

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $x_{n+1} = \dfrac{x_n^{2} + 4}{2 x_n}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 3.5000 | $\dfrac{12.25 + 4}{7} = 2.3214$ |
| 1 | 2.3214 | $\dfrac{5.389 + 4}{4.6429} = 2.0200$ |
| 2 | 2.0200 | $\dfrac{4.0804 + 4}{4.0400} = 2.0002$ |
| 3 | 2.0002 | $\dfrac{4.0008 + 4}{4.0004} = 2.0000$ |

*(4 marks for iteration table)*

**Estimate of $\lambda$** (3 marks):
Errors: $e_0 = 1.5$, $e_1 = 0.3214$, $e_2 = 0.0200$, $e_3 = 0.0002$.

$$
\dfrac{\vert e_1 \vert}{\vert e_0 \vert} = 0.214, \quad \dfrac{\vert e_2 \vert}{\vert e_1 \vert} = 0.062, \quad \dfrac{\vert e_3 \vert}{\vert e_2 \vert} = 0.010
$$

The ratios fall **toward zero**, confirming that $g_1'(2) = 0$ gives **super-linear (quadratic)** convergence, with $\lambda \to 0$.

$$\boxed{\,\text{Convergent scheme: } g_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 4}{2x};\quad \lambda \approx 0\,}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose No Marks!**
> 1. **Forgetting the convergence test** before iterating: Examiners dock 1–2 marks if $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ is never checked *(Most common in Part A)*.
> 2. **Substituting $g$ into itself** with arithmetic slips in $x_{n+1}$. Carry **5 decimal places** until the convergence step.
> 3. **Wrong fixed point identification:** When $f(x) = x^2 - 4$, students sometimes conclude "$\alpha = 2$" but iterate towards a different fixed point because they chose a $g$ whose fixed point is not a root of $f$. Always verify.
> 4. **Mixing the order of convergence** with the rate constant. $p=1$ (linear) does *not* mean slow — the *magnitude* of $g'(\alpha)$ controls speed.
> 5. **Omitting the "why"**: In 14-mark questions, every formula must be supported by a one-line "explanation of conversion logic" — examiners want to see comprehension, not just computation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to remember at a glance:**

- **FPI is open-bracket:** No bracketing of root needed, unlike Bisection.
- **Recurrence:** $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ where $\alpha = g(\alpha) \equiv f(\alpha)=0$.
- **Convergence needs:** $g$ continuous, $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$ near $\alpha$.
- **Order of convergence:** $p = 1$ (linear) when $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$; quadratic if $g'(\alpha) = 0$.
- **Error relation:** $e_{n+1} \approx g'(\alpha)\,e_n$ — error contracts by factor $g'(\alpha)$ each step.
- **Asymptotic constant:** $\lambda = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$ (and the smaller, the better).
- **Termination criteria:** $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ (absolute) **or** $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$ (relative).
- **Negative $g'(\alpha)$:** Causes *oscillating* (sign-alternating) iterates — still converges if $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$.
- **Divergence triggers:** $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert > 1$, or 2-cycle trap when $g'(\alpha) = -1$.
- **Multiple valid $g(x)$:** Engineering art = pick $g$ such that $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ is minimised.
- **Iteration count formula:** $n > \dfrac{\ln(\varepsilon / \vert e_0 \vert)}{\ln \vert g'(\alpha) \vert}$.
- **Error bound (a-priori):** $\vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1-k}\,\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$.
- **Caveat — fixed point vs root:** A $g$ may have a fixed point that is *not* a root of $f$ — always substitute back to check.
- **Engineering use cases:** ADC design, LDPC decoding, PID steady-state, CFD SIMPLE algorithm, structural equilibrium solvers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convergence and Error Analysis of Root-Finding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Convergence and Error Analysis of Root-Finding Methods # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Convergence in Root-Finding? In numerical analysis, a root-finding method is an iterative algorithm that generates a sequence of approximations $x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n, \ldots$ to a true root $\alpha$ of the equation $f(x) = 0$. The method is said to **converge** if: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = \alpha$$ equivalently, the sequence of absolute errors $e_n = \vert x_n - \alpha \vert$ approaches zero as $n \to \infty$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A root-finding method is *convergent* on an interval $[a, b]$ if the sequence of iterates $\{x_n\}$ approaches the true root $\alpha$ such that $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} \vert x_n - \alpha \vert \to 0$ regardless of the initial guess $x_0$ chosen in a suitable neighborhood of $\alpha$. ### 1.2 Order of Convergence (KTU High-Priority Concept) The **order of convergence** of a sequence $\{x_n\}$ converging to $\alpha$ is the largest positive number $p$ such that: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = C \neq 0, \quad \text{where } C \text{ is the asymptotic error constant.}$$ - If $p = 1$ and $0 < C < 1$ → **Linear Convergence** - If $p = 2$ → **Quadratic Convergence** - If $1 < p < 2$ → **Superlinear Convergence** (e.g., Secant, $p \approx 1.618$) ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Bullseye 🎯 > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine throwing darts at a dartboard to hit the bullseye (the root $\alpha$). > - **Linear convergence** is like halving the distance to the bullseye every throw — steady but slow. > - **Quadratic convergence** is like *squaring* the accuracy every throw — when you're close, you zoom in dramatically. > - The **order of convergence** is the "acceleration exponent" — higher $p$ means faster zipping toward the target. **Real-World Engineering Application:** In civil/structural engineering, finding the buckling load of a column reduces to solving a transcendental equation. The **Newton-Raphson method** (quadratic convergence) is preferred over the **bisection method** (linear) because design offices need rapid, high-precision solutions to meet tight project deadlines. ## 1.4 Standard Convergence Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Asked) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Method | |--------|--------|---------------|--------| | **Order of Convergence** | $p$ | **1, 1.618, 2** | Bisection / Secant / Newton | | **Asymptotic Error Constant** | $C$ or $\mu$ | $\left\vert \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)} \right\vert$ | Newton-Raphson | | **Convergence Rate (per step)** | $-\log_{10} C$ | digits gained per iter. | All methods | | **Iteration Count Bound** | $N \geq \log_2\left(\frac{b-a}{\epsilon}\right)$ | Bisection | Bisection | # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Order of Convergence — Formal Operational Logic The order $p$ is found using the limit definition: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = C, \quad C > 0$$ ### Operational Logic Steps (Why & How): - **Step 1 — Why use this ratio?** It measures *how the new error scales* with the *p-th power* of the old error. If $e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n^p$, then each iteration multiplies the log-error by a factor $p$, giving geometric reduction of digits. - **Step 2 — How to compute $p$ in practice?** Track three consecutive errors $e_n, e_{n+1}, e_{n+2}$ and apply the KTU-friendly log-formula: $$p \approx \frac{\ln \left( \vert e_{n+1} \vert / \vert e_n \vert \right)}{\ln \left( \vert e_{n+2} \vert / \vert e_{n+1} \vert \right)}$$ - **Step 3 — Interpretation:** - $p = 1, C < 1$: linear — number of correct digits grows arithmetically. - $p = 2$: quadratic — number of correct digits roughly *doubles* each iteration (most desirable). - $p > 1$: superlinear — faster than linear, slower than quadratic. ## 2.2 Convergence Theorems for Each Method ### 2.2.1 Bisection Method - **Convergence type:** Guaranteed, linear ($p=1$), with $C = 1/2$. - **Error bound:** $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b - a}{2^n}$ - **Iterations needed for tolerance $\epsilon$:** $$n \geq \log_2 \left( \frac{b - a}{\epsilon} \right)$$ ### 2.2.2 Fixed-Point Iteration: $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ - **Sufficient conditions** (KTU favorite): 1. $g, g' \in C[a,b]$ 2. $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$ for all $x \in [a, b]$ 3. $g(x) \in [a, b]$ for all $x \in [a, b]$ - **Error bound:** $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1 - k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$ - **Order:** $p = 1$ (linear) if $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$; $p \geq 2$ if $g'(\alpha) = 0$. ### 2.2.3 Newton-Raphson Method: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ - **Order:** $p = 2$ (quadratic). - **Asymptotic error constant:** $$C = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$$ - **Error relation:** $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \approx C \cdot e_n^2$ ### 2.2.4 Secant Method: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$ - **Order:** $p = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ (golden ratio, superlinear). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE** — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Numerical Methods board paper. | Method | Order $p$ | Asymptotic Error Constant $C$ | Error Recurrence $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq$ | Iterations for tolerance $\epsilon$ | |--------|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Bisection** | $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $\dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$ | $n \geq \log_2 \left( \dfrac{b-a}{\epsilon} \right)$ | | **Fixed-Point** | $1$ | $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert = k$ | $\dfrac{k^n}{1-k}\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$ | $n \geq \dfrac{\ln \epsilon - \ln \vert e_0 \vert}{\ln k}$ | | **Newton-Raphson** | $2$ | $\dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2\vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$ | $C \cdot e_n^2$ | $n \approx \log_2 \left( \dfrac{\ln \epsilon}{\ln \vert e_0 \vert} \right)$ | | **Secant** | $\dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$ | $\left( \dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2\vert f'(\alpha) \vert} \right)^{1/2}$ | $C \cdot e_n^{1.618}$ | superlinear — derived from log ratio | | **Regula-Falsi** | $1$ | depends on $f''$ | similar to bisection but linear | linear (slower than secant) | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Aerospace**: Solving angle-of-attack transcendental equations for wing lift coefficients uses Newton-Raphson for sub-millisecond convergence. - **Electrical (VLSI Design)**: MOSFET threshold-voltage equations solved via fixed-point iteration. - **Mechanical (Vibration Analysis)**: Natural frequency equations $k - \omega^2 m = 0$ use Secant method when derivative evaluation is expensive. - **Civil (Structural Buckling)**: Euler's column equation requires rapid root-finding; quadratic methods are mandated for real-time simulation. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Order of Convergence of Newton-Raphson Method ⭐ (Most Important — 14 marks possible) **Given:** Newton-Raphson iteration formula: $$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$ **Let** $e_n = x_n - \alpha$, so $x_n = \alpha + e_n$. We assume $f(\alpha) = 0$. **Step 1 — Taylor expansion of $f(x_n)$ around $\alpha$:** $$f(x_n) = f(\alpha + e_n) = f(\alpha) + f'(\alpha) e_n + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2!} e_n^2 + \frac{f'''(\alpha)}{3!} e_n^3 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^4)$$ Since $f(\alpha) = 0$: $$f(x_n) = f'(\alpha) e_n + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2} e_n^2 + \frac{f'''(\alpha)}{6} e_n^3 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^4)$$ **Step 2 — Taylor expansion of $f'(x_n)$ around $\alpha$:** $$f'(x_n) = f'(\alpha + e_n) = f'(\alpha) + f''(\alpha) e_n + \frac{f'''(\alpha)}{2} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio $f(x_n) / f'(x_n)$:** Divide numerator and denominator by $f'(\alpha)$: $$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \frac{e_n + \dfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \dfrac{f'''(\alpha)}{6 f'(\alpha)} e_n^3 + \cdots}{1 + \dfrac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} e_n + \dfrac{f'''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \cdots}$$ **Step 4 — Use the expansion $(1 + u)^{-1} = 1 - u + u^2 - \cdots$ for the denominator:** $$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \left( e_n + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3) \right) \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} e_n + \mathcal{O}(e_n^2) \right)$$ **Step 5 — Multiply out term by term:** $$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = e_n - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ Collect the $e_n^2$ coefficient: $$= e_n + \left( \frac{1}{2 f'(\alpha)} - \frac{1}{f'(\alpha)} \right) f''(\alpha) \, e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ $$= e_n - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back into Newton-Raphson formula:** $$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = (\alpha + e_n) - \left[ e_n - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3) \right]$$ $$x_{n+1} = \alpha + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ **Step 7 — Extract the new error $e_{n+1} = x_{n+1} - \alpha$:** $$\boxed{e_{n+1} = \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \, e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)}$$ **Step 8 — Apply the definition of order of convergence:** $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^2} = \left\vert \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \right\vert = C \neq 0$$ **Conclusion:** The Newton-Raphson method has **order of convergence $p = 2$** (quadratic) with asymptotic error constant: $$C = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight for KTU exams:** The constant $C$ quantifies *how fast* the quadratic convergence happens. Smaller $C$ ⇒ faster convergence. The number of correct decimal digits approximately **doubles** every iteration. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Bisection Method Error Bound **Given:** Bisection halts the interval $[a_n, b_n]$ with $\alpha \in [a_n, b_n]$. **Inductive claim:** $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b - a}{2^n}$ **Base case ($n = 0$):** $\vert e_0 \vert = \vert x_0 - \alpha \vert \leq b - a = \dfrac{b-a}{2^0}$ ✓ **Inductive step:** Assume $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2^n}$. After bisection, the new interval has length $\dfrac{b_n - a_n}{2} = \dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$. Since $x_{n+1}$ is the midpoint: $$\vert e_{n+1} \vert = \left\vert x_{n+1} - \alpha \right\vert \leq \frac{b_{n+1} - a_{n+1}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{b-a}{2^{n+1}} = \frac{b-a}{2^{n+2}}$$ For practical KTU purposes, we use the slightly looser bound: $$\boxed{\vert e_n \vert \leq \frac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering impact:** To guarantee accuracy $10^{-5}$ on an interval of width 1, we need only $n \geq \log_2(10^5) \approx 17$ iterations — bisection is *predictable* and *robust*. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Fixed-Point Iteration Error Bound **Given:** $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ with $\alpha = g(\alpha)$, $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$ on $[a,b]$. **Step 1 — Express error using Mean Value Theorem:** $$e_{n+1} = x_{n+1} - \alpha = g(x_n) - g(\alpha) = g'(\xi_n)(x_n - \alpha) = g'(\xi_n) e_n$$ for some $\xi_n$ between $x_n$ and $\alpha$. **Step 2 — Take absolute values:** $$\vert e_{n+1} \vert = \vert g'(\xi_n) \vert \cdot \vert e_n \vert \leq k \cdot \vert e_n \vert$$ **Step 3 — By induction:** $$\boxed{\vert e_n \vert \leq k^n \cdot \vert e_0 \vert}$$ **Step 4 — Tighter a-posteriori bound (a priori, used in KTU exams):** Subtracting consecutive errors: $$\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq k \vert e_n \vert \implies \text{using geometric series telescoping:}$$ $$\vert e_n \vert \leq \frac{k^n}{1 - k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$$ **Order of convergence:** Since $e_{n+1} = g'(\alpha) e_n + \mathcal{O}(e_n^2)$, if $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert} = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert = C < 1 \implies p = 1 \text{ (linear)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Order of Convergence of the Secant Method **Given:** $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$ Let $e_n = x_n - \alpha$, $e_{n-1} = x_{n-1} - \alpha$. **Step 1 — Taylor expansions:** $$f(x_n) = f'(\alpha) e_n + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ $$f(x_{n-1}) = f'(\alpha) e_{n-1} + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2} e_{n-1}^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_{n-1}^3)$$ **Step 2 — Form the secant slope:** $$\frac{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}{x_n - x_{n-1}} = f'(\alpha) + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2}(e_n + e_{n-1}) + \mathcal{O}(e^2)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the iteration formula and expand:** After considerable algebra (omitted for brevity but reproducible in KTU board answer scripts): $$e_{n+1} = \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n e_{n-1} + \mathcal{O}(e^3)$$ **Step 4 — Assume power-law form $e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n^p$ with $e_{n-1} \approx C \cdot e_{n-1}^{1/p} \cdot e_{n-1}$ — actually use $e_{n-1} = C^{1/p} e_n^{1/p}$.** Solving self-consistently: $$C \cdot e_n^p \approx C \cdot e_n \cdot e_{n-1} \implies e_{n-1} = C^{1/p} e_n^{1/p}$$ Substituting back: $e_{n+1} = C \cdot e_n \cdot C^{1/p} e_n^{1/p} = C^{1 + 1/p} e_n^{1 + 1/p}$, so $p = 1 + \dfrac{1}{p}$, giving: $$\boxed{p^2 - p - 1 = 0 \implies p = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618}$$ This is the **golden ratio** — the Secant method is superlinear but slower than Newton-Raphson. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Estimating Iteration Count (KTU-favored problem) **Problem:** Find the minimum iterations $n$ for the **bisection method** on $[0, 1]$ to achieve an absolute error $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$. **Solution:** Apply the KTU formula: $$n \geq \log_2 \left( \frac{b - a}{\epsilon} \right) = \log_2 \left( \frac{1 - 0}{10^{-5}} \right) = \log_2(10^5)$$ $$\log_2(10^5) = 5 \log_2(10) = 5 \cdot 3.32193 = 16.6096$$ Since $n$ must be an integer: $$\boxed{n_{\min} = 17 \text{ iterations}}$$ **Cross-check:** $\dfrac{1}{2^{17}} = \dfrac{1}{131072} \approx 7.63 \times 10^{-6} < 10^{-5}$ ✓ --- ## 3.6 Comparative Convergence Matrix | Aspect | Bisection | Fixed-Point | Newton-Raphson | Secant | |--------|-----------|-------------|----------------|--------| | **Convergence Guaranteed?** | ✅ Yes | Conditional | Conditional | Conditional | | **Order $p$** | $1$ | $1$ | $2$ | $1.618$ | | **Function evaluations/iter** | $1$ | $1$ | $2$ ($f$ and $f'$) | $1$ (reuses $f(x_{n-1})$) | | **Derivative Required?** | No | No | **Yes** | No | | **Starting requirement** | Bracket $f(a)f(b) < 0$ | $\vert g'(x)\vert < 1$ | Close to $\alpha$ | Two initial guesses | | **Error bound formula** | $\dfrac{b-a}{2^n}$ | $\dfrac{k^n}{1-k}\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$ | $C \cdot e_n^2$ | $C \cdot e_n^{1.618}$ | # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Convergence Behavior Comparison — Functional Architecture Flow The diagram below maps the **decision logic flow** a numerical analyst uses to select and validate a root-finding method based on convergence properties. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: f x equals 0 on interval a b] --> B{Is f continuous
and f of a times f of b less than 0?} B -- Yes --> C[Bisection Method] B -- No --> D{Rewrite as x equals g of x
with g prime of alpha less than 1?} D -- Yes --> E[Fixed-Point Iteration] D -- No --> F{Is f prime of x
easily computable?} F -- Yes --> G[Newton-Raphson Method] F -- No --> H{Do we have two
initial guesses?} H -- Yes --> I[Secant Method] H -- No --> J[Return to bracketing
or reformulation] C --> K[Track error e sub n] E --> K G --> K I --> K K --> L{Is absolute error
less than epsilon?} L -- No --> M[Generate next iterate x sub n plus 1] M --> K L -- Yes --> N[Stop: alpha is the root] N --> O[Report order p
and asymptotic error C] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Error Propagation in Iterative Methods This schematic illustrates how the *asymptotic error constant $C$* and the *order $p$* jointly govern the rate at which $\vert e_n \vert$ shrinks. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Current Error e sub n] --> B[Apply Iteration Function Phi] B --> C[Compute New Error e sub n plus 1] C --> D{Apply Error Recurrence:
absolute value of e sub n plus 1
approximately equals
C times absolute value of e sub n to power p} D --> E[Update Iteration Counter] E --> F{Convergence Test:
absolute value of e sub n plus 1
less than tolerance epsilon} F -- No --> A F -- Yes --> G[Output Approximate Root alpha hat] H[Order p and Constant C] --> D ``` ## 4.3 Visual Comparison: Convergence Speeds (Mermaid Plot Simulation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LINEAR [Linear Convergence - p equals 1] L1[Iteration 0: error 1.0] --> L2[Iteration 1: error 0.50] L2 --> L3[Iteration 2: error 0.25] L3 --> L4[Iteration 3: error 0.125] L4 --> L5[Iteration 4: error 0.0625] end subgraph QUAD [Quadratic Convergence - p equals 2] Q1[Iteration 0: error 1.0] --> Q2[Iteration 1: error 0.50] Q2 --> Q3[Iteration 2: error 0.0625] Q3 --> Q4[Iteration 3: error 0.00098] Q4 --> Q5[Iteration 4: error 2.4 times 10 to power minus 7] end LINEAR --> COMPARE QUAD --> COMPARE COMPARE[Quadratic: ~16 digits in 4 steps
Linear: ~4 digits in 4 steps] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Notice that quadratic convergence provides **4 correct decimal digits after just 2 iterations**, whereas bisection only provides 2 digits in 2 iterations. This is why Newton-Raphson is the workhorse of engineering numerical software. # 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]` **Define order of convergence of an iterative method. The Newton-Raphson method has order of convergence equal to ______. State the asymptotic error constant.** **Model Answer:** The order of convergence $p$ of a sequence $\{x_n\}$ converging to $\alpha$ is the largest positive integer such that: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = C \neq 0$$ where $e_n = \vert x_n - \alpha \vert$ and $C$ is the asymptotic error constant. - **Order of Newton-Raphson:** $p = 2$ (quadratic) - **Asymptotic error constant:** $C = \dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$ **Marking scheme (KTU pattern):** Definition (1) + value of $p$ (1) + constant (1) = 3 marks. --- ### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]` **State and prove the error bound for the bisection method. How many iterations are needed to find a root in $[0, 1]$ with accuracy $10^{-6}$?** **Model Answer:** **Error Bound:** If $\alpha \in [a, b]$ and $f(a) f(b) < 0$, then the $n$-th iterate $x_n$ satisfies: $$\vert e_n \vert = \vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{b - a}{2^n}$$ **Proof sketch (by induction):** - **Base case** ($n=1$): $\vert e_1 \vert \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2}$ since $x_1 = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$. - **Inductive step:** Assume $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2^n}$. The new interval after $n$ bisections has length $\dfrac{b-a}{2^n}$, so the $(n+1)$-th midpoint $x_{n+1}$ satisfies $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$. **Iteration count for $a=0, b=1, \epsilon = 10^{-6}$:** $$n \geq \log_2\left(\frac{1}{10^{-6}}\right) = 6 \log_2(10) = 6 \times 3.32193 = 19.93$$ $$\boxed{n_{\min} = 20 \text{ iterations}}$$ --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE --- ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]` **(a)** *Derive the order of convergence of the Newton-Raphson method. Show that the asymptotic error constant is $\dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$.* **[7 marks]** **(b)** *Apply Newton-Raphson to find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ starting from $x_0 = 2$ for 3 iterations. Verify the quadratic convergence using error ratios.* **[7 marks]** --- #### 🔑 Model Solution to A(a): **Step 1:** Newton-Raphson formula: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ **Step 2:** Let $e_n = x_n - \alpha$, with $f(\alpha) = 0$. **Step 3:** Taylor expansions around $\alpha$: $$f(x_n) = f'(\alpha) e_n + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2} e_n^2 + \frac{f'''(\alpha)}{6} e_n^3 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^4)$$ $$f'(x_n) = f'(\alpha) + f''(\alpha) e_n + \frac{f'''(\alpha)}{2} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ **Step 4:** Form the ratio and expand: $$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \frac{e_n + \dfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \cdots}{1 + \dfrac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} e_n + \cdots}$$ **Step 5:** Using $(1+u)^{-1} \approx 1 - u + u^2 - \cdots$: $$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \left( e_n + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 \right) \left( 1 - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} e_n \right) + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ $$= e_n + \left( \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)} - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} \right) e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3) = e_n - \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ **Step 6:** Subtract: $$e_{n+1} = x_{n+1} - \alpha = e_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)} e_n^2 + \mathcal{O}(e_n^3)$$ **Step 7:** Conclusion: $p = 2$ (quadratic) with $C = \dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$. --- #### 🔑 Model Solution to A(b): **Given:** $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$, $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$, $x_0 = 2$. **True root** (for verification): $\alpha \approx 2.0945515$ | Iter $n$ | $x_n$ | $f(x_n)$ | $f'(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1} = x_n - f/f'$ | $e_n = \vert x_n - \alpha\vert$ | |----------|-------|----------|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------| | $0$ | $2.000000$ | $-1.000000$ | $10.000000$ | $2.100000$ | $0.0945515$ | | $1$ | $2.100000$ | $0.061000$ | $11.230000$ | $2.094568$ | $0.005449$ | | $2$ | $2.094568$ | $\approx 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | $11.1607$ | $2.0945515$ | $1.66 \times 10^{-5}$ | **Verification of quadratic convergence:** $$\frac{\vert e_1 \vert}{\vert e_0 \vert^2} = \frac{0.005449}{(0.0945515)^2} = \frac{0.005449}{0.008940} \approx 0.6095$$ $$\frac{\vert e_2 \vert}{\vert e_1 \vert^2} = \frac{1.66 \times 10^{-5}}{(0.005449)^2} = \frac{1.66 \times 10^{-5}}{2.969 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.559$$ **Theoretical constant:** $$C = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert} = \frac{\vert 2\alpha \vert}{2 \vert 3\alpha^2 - 2 \vert} = \frac{2 \times 2.0946}{2 \times 11.16} = \frac{4.189}{22.32} \approx 0.188$$ Both ratios approach a constant $C \approx 0.188$ as $n$ grows — **quadratic convergence confirmed**. The 3rd-iteration error is $1.66 \times 10^{-5}$, achieving 5 correct decimal digits. ✓ --- ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** *Explain the convergence criteria for fixed-point iteration. State and prove the error bound $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1-k}\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert$.* **[7 marks]** **(b)** *Compare the order of convergence and computational cost of Bisection, Newton-Raphson, and Secant methods using a table. Use the equation $f(x) = \cos(x) - x$ on $[0, 1]$ to illustrate that Newton-Raphson requires fewer iterations than Bisection for the same tolerance.* **[7 marks]** --- #### 🔑 Model Solution to B(a): **Theorem (Fixed-Point Convergence):** Let $g: [a, b] \to [a, b]$ be continuously differentiable with $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$ for all $x \in [a,b]$. Then for any $x_0 \in [a,b]$, the iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ converges to a unique fixed point $\alpha \in [a,b]$. **Error bound derivation:** **Step 1:** $e_{n+1} = x_{n+1} - \alpha = g(x_n) - g(\alpha)$ By Mean Value Theorem: $g(x_n) - g(\alpha) = g'(\xi_n) e_n$ for some $\xi_n$ between $x_n$ and $\alpha$. $$\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq k \vert e_n \vert$$ **Step 2:** Telescoping product: $$\vert e_n \vert \leq k^n \vert e_0 \vert = k^n \vert x_0 - \alpha \vert$$ **Step 3 (a priori bound):** Use $\vert x_1 - x_0 \vert \geq (1-k)\vert x_0 - \alpha \vert$ (because $\vert x_0 - \alpha \vert = \vert x_0 - x_1 + x_1 - \alpha \vert \leq \vert x_0 - x_1 \vert + \vert x_1 - \alpha \vert \leq \vert x_0 - x_1 \vert + k \vert x_0 - \alpha \vert$). Solving: $$(1-k)\vert x_0 - \alpha \vert \leq \vert x_0 - x_1 \vert \implies \vert x_0 - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{\vert x_0 - x_1 \vert}{1-k}$$ **Step 4:** Combine: $$\boxed{\vert e_n \vert = \vert x_n - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{k^n}{1-k} \vert x_1 - x_0 \vert}$$ **Order:** Since $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$, we have $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert} = k < 1$, so $p = 1$ (linear). --- #### 🔑 Model Solution to B(b): **Comparison table:** | Criterion | Bisection | Newton-Raphson | Secant | |-----------|-----------|----------------|--------| | Order $p$ | $1$ (linear) | $2$ (quadratic) | $1.618$ (superlinear) | | Convex. Guarantee | ✅ Always | ❌ Conditional | ❌ Conditional | | Derivative needed | No | Yes | No | | Cost per iter | $1$ eval of $f$ | $1 f + 1 f'$ | $1 f$ (reused) | | Error recurrence | $\dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$ | $C \cdot e_n^2$ | $C \cdot e_n^{1.618}$ | **Numerical illustration for $f(x) = \cos(x) - x$, $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$:** True root: $\alpha \approx 0.7390851$ **Bisection iterations on $[0,1]$:** - $n \geq \log_2(10^5) = 16.61 \implies n = 17$ iterations **Newton-Raphson iterations from $x_0 = 0.5$:** | Iter | $x_n$ | $\vert e_n \vert$ | |------|-------|------------------| | 0 | $0.500000$ | $0.239085$ | | 1 | $0.755222$ | $0.016137$ | | 2 | $0.739142$ | $5.7 \times 10^{-5}$ | | 3 | $0.739085$ | $1.2 \times 10^{-8}$ | **Total Newton-Raphson iterations to reach $10^{-5}$:** $n = 3$ **Comparison:** Newton-Raphson needs only **3 iterations** vs. bisection's **17 iterations** — a **5.7× speedup** for the same accuracy, despite Newton-Raphson having higher per-iteration cost. **Computational cost (function evaluations):** - Bisection: $17 \times 1 = 17$ evaluations - Newton-Raphson: $3 \times 2 = 6$ evaluations (each iter evaluates both $f$ and $f'$) > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Do not confuse order $p$ with convergence rate.** Order $p = 2$ means errors square; convergence *rate* $-\log_{10} C$ measures digit gain. > 2. **Bisection error bound is $\dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$** (with $n+1$ in denominator) — many students write $2^n$ and lose 1 mark. > 3. **For Newton-Raphson derivation, do NOT skip the Taylor expansion step** — this alone is worth 3 of 7 marks. > 4. **Secant method order $1.618$ comes from solving $p^2 = p + 1$** — memorize this derivation. > 5. **Always state assumptions** ($f$ continuous, $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$) before claiming quadratic convergence. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE** ### 📌 Core Definitions - ✅ **Order of convergence $p$:** largest $p$ such that $\lim \dfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = C > 0$ - ✅ **Asymptotic error constant $C$:** limit of ratio above (determines speed at order $p$) - ✅ **Linear convergence:** $p = 1$, $0 < C < 1$ — digits grow arithmetically - ✅ **Quadratic convergence:** $p = 2$ — digits roughly **double** each iteration - ✅ **Superlinear:** $1 < p < 2$ — e.g., Secant's $p = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ ### 📌 Method-Specific Order & Error Recurrence - ✅ **Bisection:** $p = 1$, $C = 1/2$, $\vert e_n \vert \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$ - ✅ **Fixed-Point:** $p = 1$ if $g'(\alpha) \neq 0$, $C = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert = k$ - ✅ **Newton-Raphson:** $p = 2$, $C = \dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2\vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$, $e_{n+1} = C \cdot e_n^2$ - ✅ **Secant:** $p = 1.618$ (golden ratio), $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2\vert f'(\alpha) \vert}}$ ### 📌 Iteration Count Formulas - ✅ **Bisection:** $n \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{b-a}{\epsilon}\right)$ - ✅ **Newton-Raphson:** $n \geq \log_2 \left( \dfrac{\log \epsilon}{\log \vert e_0 \vert} \right)$ (approximate) ### 📌 Key Conditions for Convergence - ✅ **Bisection:** $f$ continuous, $f(a) f(b) < 0$ (guaranteed) - ✅ **Fixed-Point:** $g \in C^1$, $\vert g'(x) \vert \leq k < 1$, $g([a,b]) \subseteq [a,b]$ - ✅ **Newton-Raphson:** $f \in C^2$, $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$, $x_0$ close to $\alpha$ - ✅ **Secant:** Two initial guesses $x_0, x_1$ near $\alpha$, $f''$ continuous ### 📌 Common Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Confusing $C$ with $k$ (they are different constants in different methods) - ❌ Forgetting that Newton-Raphson may **diverge** if $x_0$ is far from $\alpha$ - ❌ Writing $e_n \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2^n}$ instead of $2^{n+1}$ for bisection - ❌ Skipping the Taylor expansion derivation in part (a) of 14-mark questions - ❌ Applying the wrong error formula for secant vs. Newton-Raphson ### 📌 Engineering Applications - 🏗️ **Civil:** Buckling load of columns (Euler's transcendental equation) - ✈️ **Aerospace:** Wing lift curve, angle-of-attack - ⚡ **Electrical:** MOSFET threshold voltage, RL/RC circuit resonance - 🏭 **Mechanical:** Natural frequency of vibrating systems > 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** For any 14-mark question on convergence, follow the structure: **(1) State the method, (2) Taylor expansion (if applicable), (3) Derive error recurrence, (4) Apply limit definition of $p$, (5) Conclude with order and constant.** This guarantees full marks!

topics.title: Solving Nonlinear Equations Using the Above Methods in Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solving Nonlinear Equations Using the Above Methods in Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Nonlinear Equation?

A **nonlinear equation** is an equation in which the unknown variable $x$ appears with a degree greater than one, inside transcendental functions (like $\sin$, $\cos$, $\exp$, $\log$), or in any combination that prevents a closed-form analytical solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (PEICT524 - Module 1):**
> A nonlinear equation in one variable is an equation of the form $f(x) = 0$, where $f(x)$ is a real-valued continuous function that is not linear in $x$. Examples include polynomial equations of degree $\geq 2$, transcendental equations like $e^x - 3x = 0$, and trigonometric equations like $\cos(x) - x = 0$.
