topics.title: Error Analysis in Numerical Differentiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Error Analysis in Numerical Differentiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Error Analysis in Numerical Differentiation** is the systematic study of the deviation between the *exact analytical derivative* $f'(x)$ of a function and its *numerical approximation* obtained via finite-difference quotients. These deviations arise because we replace the infinitesimal limit definition of a derivative with a finite algebraic expression evaluated on a discrete mesh of step size $h > 0$.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic requires the student to:
- Decompose the numerical differentiation error into **truncation error** and **round-off error**.
- Determine the **order of accuracy** of common finite-difference formulas.
- Compute the **optimal step size** $h_{opt}$ that minimizes the total error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Definition (Use Verbatim):**
> *Numerical differentiation is the process of computing the value of the derivative of a function using its values at a finite set of points. The error in such an approximation consists of two parts — the truncation error (discretization error) caused by replacing the derivative with a finite difference, and the round-off error caused by finite arithmetic precision in computing the difference quotient.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Speedometer in a Tunnel"**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car through a long, pitch-dark tunnel and your dashboard speedometer has failed. To estimate your *instantaneous speed* at the tunnel entrance, you time yourself over a short measured stretch of length $h$:
> $$\text{Speed} \approx \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{h}{\Delta t}$$
>
> - If you choose a **very small $h$** (say 1 metre), the timer becomes unreliable because it cannot resolve tiny time intervals — this is **round-off error**.
> - If you choose a **very large $h$** (say 1 km), your average speed no longer represents the speed at the exact entry point — this is **truncation error**.
>
> Somewhere in between lies an **optimal $h$** that gives the best estimate. Numerical differentiation behaves exactly the same way.

## 1.3 Classification of Errors

The two canonical error families studied under this topic are:

| Error Type | Symbol | Cause | Behaviour as $h \to 0$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Truncation Error** | $E_T$ | Replacing limit with finite difference | Decreases (often as $O(h^k)$) |
| **Round-off Error** | $E_R$ | Finite word-length in arithmetic | Increases (typically as $O(1/h)$) |
| **Total Error** | $E_{total}$ | Sum of both | Has a well-defined minimum |

The central engineering insight is that simply choosing $h$ smaller is **not** always beneficial — beyond a certain threshold, round-off begins to dominate and the answer becomes *worse*, not better.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Source Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Truncation Error (Discretization Error)

By Taylor's theorem, expanding $f(x+h)$ and $f(x-h)$ about $x$:

$$
f(x+h) = f(x) + hf'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2}f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24}f^{(4)}(x) + O(h^5)
$$

$$
f(x-h) = f(x) - hf'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2}f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24}f^{(4)}(x) + O(h^5)
$$

Because we drop the higher-order terms to form a finite-difference quotient, the part we discard is the **truncation error**.

### 2.1.2 Round-off Error

Each function evaluation $f(x \pm h)$ is stored with at most $t$ significant binary digits. The absolute representation error is bounded by a machine epsilon $\varepsilon$, meaning:

$$
\text{fl}[f(x \pm h)] = f(x \pm h)(1 + \delta_{\pm}), \quad \vert \delta_{\pm} \vert \le \varepsilon
$$

When two such values are subtracted in the numerator of the difference quotient, the absolute error in the result is approximately $\frac{2\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{2h}$, which **grows** as $h$ shrinks — the famous phenomenon of **catastrophic cancellation**.

## 2.2 Structured Logic of Error Decomposition

- **Step 1** — Approximate $f'(x)$ using a finite-difference quotient $D(h) = \dfrac{\Delta f}{h}$.
- **Step 2** — Express $D(h)$ via Taylor series; the leftover terms constitute $E_T$.
- **Step 3** — Bound the floating-point perturbation of each $f(\cdot)$ value; this yields $E_R$.
- **Step 4** — Total error $E(h) = \vert E_T(h) \vert + \vert E_R(h) \vert$.
- **Step 5** — Minimize $E(h)$ w.r.t. $h$ to obtain $h_{opt}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU Board questions on this topic. **Memorize the order column.**

| Formula Name | Approximation Formula | Truncation Error $E_T$ | Order | Round-off Error $E_R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Difference (1st deriv.) | $D_f = \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$ | $-\dfrac{h}{2}f''(\xi)$ | $O(h)$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}$ |
| Backward Difference (1st deriv.) | $D_b = \dfrac{f(x)-f(x-h)}{h}$ | $+\dfrac{h}{2}f''(\xi)$ | $O(h)$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}$ |
| Central Difference (1st deriv.) | $D_c = \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}$ | $-\dfrac{h^2}{6}f'''(\xi)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}$ |
| 3-Point Forward (1st deriv.) | $\dfrac{-3f(x)+4f(x+h)-f(x+2h)}{2h}$ | $+\dfrac{h^2}{3}f'''(\xi)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $\dfrac{4\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}$ |
| 3-Point Backward (1st deriv.) | $\dfrac{3f(x)-4f(x-h)+f(x-2h)}{2h}$ | $+\dfrac{h^2}{3}f'''(\xi)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $\dfrac{4\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}$ |
| 5-Point Central (1st deriv.) | $\dfrac{f(x-2h)-8f(x-h)+8f(x+h)-f(x+2h)}{12h}$ | $+\dfrac{h^4}{30}f^{(5)}(\xi)$ | $O(h^4)$ | $\dfrac{9\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}$ |
| Central Difference (2nd deriv.) | $D_{c2} = \dfrac{f(x+h)-2f(x)+f(x-h)}{h^2}$ | $-\dfrac{h^2}{12}f^{(4)}(\xi)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $\dfrac{2\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h^2}$ |

### 2.4 Optimal Step Size Formulas

| Scheme | Truncation Form | Round-off Form | Optimal $h_{opt}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward / Backward (1st deriv.) | $\dfrac{h}{2} \vert f'' \vert$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h}$ | $h_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f''(x) \vert}}$ |
| Central (1st deriv.) | $\dfrac{h^2}{6} \vert f''' \vert$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h}$ | $h_{opt} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f'''(x) \vert}}$ |
| Central (2nd deriv.) | $\dfrac{h^2}{12} \vert f^{(4)} \vert$ | $\dfrac{2\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h^2}$ | $h_{opt} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{24\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f^{(4)}(x) \vert}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\sqrt[3]{\cdot}$ for central difference?**
> Because truncation decays as $h^2$ while round-off grows as $1/h$, equating them and solving gives $h^3 \propto \varepsilon$, hence the cube root. This non-integer exponent frequently appears in KTU Board problems and is a *very common valuation trap* if you write $\sqrt{\cdot}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace CFD:** Computing local Mach number gradients on a coarse mesh — choosing $h$ too small tanks the simulation with round-off.
- **Structural FEM:** Estimating strain from nodal displacement data — the 5-point central scheme is used for $O(h^4)$ accuracy on smooth stress fields.
- **Signal Processing:** Numerical differentiation of noisy sensor data (accelerometer, gyroscope) — round-off dominates at high sampling rates.
- **Computer Graphics:** Estimating surface normals from height-map data — trade-off dictates mesh resolution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic manipulation below is fully expanded. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Difference Truncation Error

**Goal:** Show that $D_f = \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$ has truncation error $-\dfrac{h}{2}f''(\xi)$.

**Step 1.** Expand $f(x+h)$ using Taylor's theorem around $x$:

$$
f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + O(h^4)
$$

**Step 2.** Subtract $f(x)$ from both sides:

$$
f(x+h) - f(x) = h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + O(h^4)
$$

**Step 3.** Divide the entire expression by $h$:

$$
\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} = f'(x) + \frac{h}{2} f''(x) + \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) + O(h^3)
$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the exact derivative $f'(x)$:

$$
f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{h}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - O(h^3)
$$

**Step 5.** Identify the *approximation* and the *leftover*:

$$
\underbrace{\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}}_{\text{Forward Difference } D_f} = f'(x) + \underbrace{\left( - \frac{h}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - \cdots \right)}_{\text{Truncation Error } E_T}
$$

**Step 6.** By the **Mean Value Theorem** for some $\xi \in (x, x+h)$, the leading term dominates:

$$
E_T = -\frac{h}{2} f''(\xi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E_T = O(h)}
$$

The forward difference formula is therefore **first-order accurate**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Central Difference Truncation Error

**Goal:** Show that $D_c = \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}$ has truncation error $-\dfrac{h^2}{6}f'''(\xi)$.

**Step 1.** Write the Taylor expansions of $f(x+h)$ and $f(x-h)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x+h) &= f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)}(x) + O(h^5) \\
f(x-h) &= f(x) - h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)}(x) + O(h^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Subtract $f(x-h)$ from $f(x+h)$. The even-power terms cancel:

$$
f(x+h) - f(x-h) = 2h f'(x) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^5}{120} f^{(5)}(x) + O(h^7)
$$

**Step 3.** Simplify the coefficients:

$$
f(x+h) - f(x-h) = 2h f'(x) + \frac{h^3}{3} f'''(x) + \frac{h^5}{60} f^{(5)}(x) + O(h^7)
$$

**Step 4.** Divide both sides by $2h$:

$$
\frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} = f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{120} f^{(5)}(x) + O(h^6)
$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $f'(x)$:

$$
f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - O(h^4)
$$

**Step 6.** Identify the truncation error and apply the Mean Value Theorem:

$$
E_T = -\frac{h^2}{6} f'''(\xi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E_T = O(h^2)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The central difference is **second-order accurate** because the leading $O(h)$ term (which appears in forward/backward) cancels due to symmetry. This cancellation is the entire reason central differences are preferred in smooth regions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 5-Point Central Difference Truncation Error

**Goal:** Show that $D_5 = \dfrac{f(x-2h) - 8f(x-h) + 8f(x+h) - f(x+2h)}{12h}$ has truncation error $\dfrac{h^4}{30}f^{(5)}(\xi)$.

**Step 1.** Write Taylor expansions at $\pm h$ and $\pm 2h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x+h)  &= f + h f' + \tfrac{h^2}{2} f'' + \tfrac{h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)} + \tfrac{h^5}{120} f^{(5)} + O(h^6) \\
f(x-h)  &= f - h f' + \tfrac{h^2}{2} f'' - \tfrac{h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)} - \tfrac{h^5}{120} f^{(5)} + O(h^6) \\
f(x+2h) &= f + 2h f' + 2h^2 f'' + \tfrac{8h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{16 h^4}{24} f^{(4)} + \tfrac{32 h^5}{120} f^{(5)} + O(h^6) \\
f(x-2h) &= f - 2h f' + 2h^2 f'' - \tfrac{8h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{16 h^4}{24} f^{(4)} - \tfrac{32 h^5}{120} f^{(5)} + O(h^6)
\end{aligned}
$$

For brevity, $f \equiv f(x)$, $f' \equiv f'(x)$, and so on.

**Step 2.** Compute the weighted sum $S = f(x-2h) - 8 f(x-h) + 8 f(x+h) - f(x+2h)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \bigl[f - 2hf' + 2h^2 f'' - \tfrac{8h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{16h^4}{24} f^{(4)} - \tfrac{32h^5}{120} f^{(5)}\bigr] \\
&\quad - 8\bigl[f - hf' + \tfrac{h^2}{2} f'' - \tfrac{h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)} - \tfrac{h^5}{120} f^{(5)}\bigr] \\
&\quad + 8\bigl[f + hf' + \tfrac{h^2}{2} f'' + \tfrac{h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)} + \tfrac{h^5}{120} f^{(5)}\bigr] \\
&\quad - \bigl[f + 2hf' + 2h^2 f'' + \tfrac{8h^3}{6} f''' + \tfrac{16h^4}{24} f^{(4)} + \tfrac{32h^5}{120} f^{(5)}\bigr] + O(h^6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Collect coefficients term-by-term.

- Constant terms: $(1 - 8 + 8 - 1) f = 0$ ✓
- $f'$ terms: $(-2h + 8h + 8h - 2h) f' = 12 h f'$
- $f''$ terms: $(2h^2 - 4h^2 + 4h^2 - 2h^2) f'' = 0$ ✓
- $f'''$ terms: $\bigl(-\tfrac{8}{6} + \tfrac{8}{6} + \tfrac{8}{6} - \tfrac{8}{6}\bigr) h^3 f''' = 0$ ✓
- $f^{(4)}$ terms: $\bigl(\tfrac{16}{24} - \tfrac{8}{24} + \tfrac{8}{24} - \tfrac{16}{24}\bigr) h^4 f^{(4)} = 0$ ✓
- $f^{(5)}$ terms: $\bigl(-\tfrac{32}{120} + \tfrac{8}{120} + \tfrac{8}{120} - \tfrac{32}{120}\bigr) h^5 f^{(5)} = -\tfrac{48}{120} h^5 f^{(5)} = -\tfrac{2 h^5}{5} f^{(5)}$

**Step 4.** Therefore:

$$
S = 12 h f'(x) - \frac{2 h^5}{5} f^{(5)}(x) + O(h^6)
$$

**Step 5.** Divide by $12h$:

$$
\frac{S}{12h} = f'(x) - \frac{h^4}{30} f^{(5)}(x) + O(h^6)
$$

**Step 6.** Truncation error:

$$
\boxed{E_T = \frac{h^4}{30} f^{(5)}(\xi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad O(h^4)}
$$

The 5-point central scheme is therefore **fourth-order accurate**, the highest practical order for a 5-point stencil.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optimal Step Size for Forward Difference

**Goal:** Find $h_{opt}$ minimizing total error $E(h) = \dfrac{h}{2} \vert f'' \vert + \dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h}$.

**Step 1.** Truncation error magnitude (assuming $\vert f''(x) \vert$ bounded):

$$
E_T(h) = \frac{h}{2} \vert f''(x) \vert
$$

**Step 2.** Round-off error magnitude in computing $f(x+h) - f(x)$:

$$
E_R(h) = \frac{2 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{2h} = \frac{\varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{h}
$$

(The factor of 2 in the numerator is because two evaluations are perturbed; division by $2h$ leaves the $h$-dependence.)

**Step 3.** Total error:

$$
E(h) = \frac{h}{2} \vert f'' \vert + \frac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h}
$$

**Step 4.** Minimize by setting $\dfrac{dE}{dh} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dE}{dh} = \frac{1}{2} \vert f'' \vert - \frac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h^2} = 0
$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $h$:

$$
\frac{h^2}{2} = \frac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{\vert f'' \vert} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h^2 = \frac{2 \varepsilon \vert f \vert}{\vert f'' \vert}
$$

**Step 6.** Take the square root:

$$
\boxed{h_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f''(x) \vert}}}
$$

**Step 7.** Substitute $h_{opt}$ back into $E(h)$ to obtain the **minimum achievable error**:

$$
E_{min} = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert \cdot \vert f''(x) \vert}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Optimal Step Size for Central Difference

**Goal:** Find $h_{opt}$ for the central scheme.

**Step 1.** Truncation: $E_T(h) = \dfrac{h^2}{6} \vert f''' \vert$.

**Step 2.** Round-off: $E_R(h) = \dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h}$ (same as forward, since numerator still contains two perturbed values).

**Step 3.** Total: $E(h) = \dfrac{h^2}{6} \vert f''' \vert + \dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h}$.

**Step 4.** Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dE}{dh} = \frac{h}{3} \vert f''' \vert - \frac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h^2} = 0
$$

**Step 5.** Solve:

$$
h^3 = \frac{3 \varepsilon \vert f \vert}{\vert f''' \vert}
$$

**Step 6.** Cube root:

$$
\boxed{h_{opt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{3 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f'''(x) \vert}}}
$$

**Step 7.** Minimum total error:

$$
E_{min} = \frac{3}{2} \left( \frac{3 \varepsilon^2 \vert f \vert^2 \vert f''' \vert}{6} \right)^{1/3}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Illustration

**Worked Example.** Let $f(x) = e^x$, evaluate at $x = 1$ using forward difference with double-precision $\varepsilon = 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$.

| Step | Quantity | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $f(1)$ | Exact | $2.718281828$ |
| $f''(1)$ | Exact | $2.718281828$ |
| $h_{opt}$ | $\sqrt{2 \cdot 2.22\!\times\!10^{-16} \cdot 2.718 / 2.718}$ | $6.66 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| $E_{min}$ | $\sqrt{2 \cdot 2.22\!\times\!10^{-16} \cdot 2.718^2}$ | $1.83 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| $f'(1)$ exact | — | $2.718281828$ |
| Best forward-difference estimate | — | $2.718281\,810$ |

The minimum achievable error of $\sim 1.8 \times 10^{-8}$ is **two orders of magnitude larger** than machine epsilon — a vivid demonstration that *numerical differentiation is inherently ill-conditioned*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ill-Conditioning of Numerical Differentiation:**
> Even at the optimal $h$, the error in numerical differentiation is bounded below by a quantity proportional to $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$ (forward/backward) or $\varepsilon^{2/3}$ (central). This is why numerical differentiation is called an **ill-posed operation**: small input perturbations (round-off) produce disproportionately large output errors.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Error Composition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exact Derivative f prime of x] --> B[Finite Difference Quotient D of h]
    B --> C{Truncation Error E sub T}
    B --> D{Roundoff Error E sub R}
    C --> E[Decreases as h increases]
    D --> F[Increases as h decreases]
    E --> G[Total Error E of h]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Minimize dE over dh equal zero]
    H --> I[Optimal Step Size h sub opt]
    I --> J[Minimum Achievable Error E sub min]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Total Error Curve (Conceptual U-Shape)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Very small h] --> Q1[Roundoff dominates]
    Q1 --> R1[Large total error]
    P2[Very large h] --> Q2[Truncation dominates]
    Q2 --> R2[Large total error]
    P3[Intermediate h] --> Q3[Optimal balance point]
    Q3 --> R3[Minimum total error]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stencil Architecture of Common Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Forward Difference Stencil] --> S1a[Uses nodes x and x plus h]
    S1a --> S1b[Order O of h]
    S2[Backward Difference Stencil] --> S2a[Uses nodes x minus h and x]
    S2a --> S2b[Order O of h]
    S3[Central Difference Stencil] --> S3a[Uses nodes x minus h and x plus h]
    S3a --> S3b[Order O of h squared]
    S4[Five Point Central Stencil] --> S4a[Uses nodes x minus 2h through x plus 2h]
    S4a --> S4b[Order O of h to the 4]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Total Error Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Function Values f at x minus 2h, x minus h, x, x plus h, x plus 2h] --> STG1[Stage 1 Function Evaluation with Machine Epsilon noise]
    STG1 --> STG2[Stage 2 Weighted Linear Combination with stencil coefficients]
    STG2 --> STG3[Stage 3 Divide by step size factor]
    STG3 --> STG4[Stage 4 Output Numerical Derivative Approximation]
    STG4 --> OUT[Output]
    STG1 -.adds.-> RND[Roundoff Error E sub R]
    STG3 -.omits.-> TRC[Truncation Error E sub T]
    RND --> SUM[Total Error]
    TRC --> SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Round-off error is **injected** at the evaluation stage (Stage 1) because function values are stored approximately.
> - Truncation error is **injected** at the division stage (Stage 3) because the limit $\lim_{h \to 0}$ has been replaced by a finite ratio.
> - The two error sources flow into the same summation node, producing the total error $E(h)$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define truncation error and round-off error in the context of numerical differentiation. How do they behave as $h \to 0$?**

**Model Answer:**

Truncation error is the error introduced when the exact derivative $f'(x)$ is replaced by a finite-difference formula; it arises from omitting higher-order terms of the Taylor series. It behaves as $E_T = O(h^k)$ where $k$ is the order of the formula (e.g., $k=1$ for forward, $k=2$ for central).

Round-off error is the error introduced due to finite arithmetic precision when storing and subtracting function values. It behaves as $E_R = O(\varepsilon/h)$ where $\varepsilon$ is the machine epsilon. As $h \to 0$, truncation error decreases while round-off error **increases**, producing a U-shaped total error curve.

*(Word limit: 60-80 words. Marks awarded for: definition of each error, correct asymptotic behaviour, mention of U-shape.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the central difference formula preferred over the forward difference formula for numerical differentiation?**

**Model Answer:**

The central difference formula,
$$D_c = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h}$$
has truncation error $O(h^2)$, whereas the forward difference formula,
$$D_f = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$
has truncation error $O(h)$. This is because the symmetric stencil causes the leading $O(h)$ term to cancel in the Taylor expansion, leaving only an $O(h^2)$ remainder. For the same value of $h$, central difference therefore yields a more accurate approximation of $f'(x)$.

*(Word limit: 50-70 words. Marks awarded for: order comparison, mention of symmetric cancellation, correct order values.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the truncation error for the central difference approximation of $f'(x)$. Hence, state the order of accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $f(x) = \sin(x)$ at $x = \pi/4$, compute the optimal step size $h_{opt}$ for the central difference formula assuming machine epsilon $\varepsilon = 10^{-16}$. Also compute the minimum total error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1.** Taylor expansions about $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x+h) &= f(x) + hf'(x) + \tfrac{h^2}{2}f''(x) + \tfrac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) + \tfrac{h^4}{24}f^{(4)}(x) + O(h^5) \\
f(x-h) &= f(x) - hf'(x) + \tfrac{h^2}{2}f''(x) - \tfrac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) + \tfrac{h^4}{24}f^{(4)}(x) + O(h^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Subtract:

$$
f(x+h) - f(x-h) = 2h f'(x) + \frac{h^3}{3} f'''(x) + O(h^5)
$$

**Step 3.** Divide by $2h$:

$$
\frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} = f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) + O(h^4)
$$

**Step 4.** Truncation error:

$$
E_T = \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(\xi), \quad \xi \in (x-h, x+h)
$$

**Step 5.** Order of accuracy: $O(h^2)$, i.e., **second-order accurate**.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1.** Required derivatives at $x = \pi/4$:

$$
f(x) = \sin(x), \quad f(\pi/4) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071068
$$

$$
f'''(x) = -\cos(x), \quad f'''(\pi/4) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.7071068
$$

So $\vert f(x) \vert = 0.7071$ and $\vert f'''(x) \vert = 0.7071$.

**Step 2.** Apply optimal step size formula for central difference:

$$
h_{opt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{3 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f'''(x) \vert}}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute:

$$
h_{opt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{3 \times 10^{-16} \times 0.7071068}{0.7071068}} = \sqrt[3]{3 \times 10^{-16}}
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate:

$$
h_{opt} = (3)^{1/3} \times 10^{-16/3} = 1.4422 \times 10^{-5.3333} = 1.4422 \times 4.6416 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{opt} \approx 6.69 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 5.** Minimum total error:

$$
E_{min} = \frac{h_{opt}^2}{6} \vert f''' \vert + \frac{\varepsilon \vert f \vert}{h_{opt}}
$$

The two terms are equal at $h_{opt}$, so:

$$
E_{min} = 2 \cdot \frac{h_{opt}^2}{6} \vert f''' \vert = \frac{h_{opt}^2}{3} \cdot 0.7071
$$

$$
E_{min} = \frac{(6.69 \times 10^{-6})^2}{3} \times 0.7071 = \frac{4.476 \times 10^{-11}}{3} \times 0.7071
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{min} \approx 1.055 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the truncation error of the forward difference approximation of $f'(x)$. Also, derive the formula for the optimal step size $h_{opt}$ by minimizing the total error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $f(x) = x^3 - 2x + 1$, evaluate $f'(1)$ using the 5-point central difference formula with $h = 0.1$. Estimate the truncation error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a)

**Step 1.** Taylor expansion:

$$
f(x+h) = f(x) + hf'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2}f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) + O(h^4)
$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange:

$$
f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{h}{2}f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{6}f'''(x) - O(h^3)
$$

**Step 3.** Truncation error:

$$
\boxed{E_T = -\frac{h}{2} f''(\xi), \quad O(h)}
$$

**Step 4.** Total error: $E(h) = \dfrac{h}{2}\vert f''\vert + \dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f\vert}{h}$.

**Step 5.** Minimize: $\dfrac{dE}{dh} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert f''\vert - \dfrac{\varepsilon \vert f\vert}{h^2} = 0$.

**Step 6.** Solve:

$$
\boxed{h_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert}{\vert f''(x) \vert}}}
$$

**Step 7.** Minimum total error:

$$
\boxed{E_{min} = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon \vert f(x) \vert \cdot \vert f''(x) \vert}}
$$

---

#### Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1.** For $f(x) = x^3 - 2x + 1$:

$$
f(0.8) = (0.8)^3 - 2(0.8) + 1 = 0.512 - 1.6 + 1 = -0.088
$$

$$
f(0.9) = (0.9)^3 - 2(0.9) + 1 = 0.729 - 1.8 + 1 = -0.071
$$

$$
f(1.0) = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0
$$

$$
f(1.1) = (1.1)^3 - 2(1.1) + 1 = 1.331 - 2.2 + 1 = 0.131
$$

$$
f(1.2) = (1.2)^3 - 2(1.2) + 1 = 1.728 - 2.4 + 1 = 0.328
$$

**Step 2.** Apply 5-point central formula:

$$
D_5 = \frac{f(0.8) - 8f(0.9) + 8f(1.1) - f(1.2)}{12 \cdot 0.1}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute values:

$$
D_5 = \frac{(-0.088) - 8(-0.071) + 8(0.131) - (0.328)}{1.2}
$$

**Step 4.** Simplify numerator step by step:

$$
-0.088 - 8(-0.071) = -0.088 + 0.568 = 0.480
$$

$$
8(0.131) - 0.328 = 1.048 - 0.328 = 0.720
$$

$$
0.480 + 0.720 = 1.200
$$

**Step 5.** Divide:

$$
D_5 = \frac{1.200}{1.2} = 1.000
$$

**Step 6.** Compare with exact: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$, so $f'(1) = 3 - 2 = 1$. The numerical estimate matches exactly because $f$ is a polynomial of degree 3 and the 5-point formula has $O(h^4)$ accuracy — for cubics the residual fifth derivative is zero.

**Step 7.** Truncation error bound:

$$
E_T = \frac{h^4}{30} f^{(5)}(\xi) = 0
$$

because $f^{(5)}(x) = 0$ identically for any cubic polynomial. Hence the result is exact (to within round-off).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $E_T$:** Many students write $E_T = +\frac{h}{2}f''$ for forward difference. Correct sign is $E_T = -\frac{h}{2}f''(\xi)$. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Wrong Exponent in $h_{opt}$ for Central Difference:** Writing $h_{opt} = \sqrt{\cdot}$ instead of $\sqrt[3]{\cdot}$. This is the *single most common* error in KTU scripts. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the Mean Value Theorem qualifier:** The truncation error is evaluated at *some* $\xi \in (x, x+h)$, not at $x$ itself. Writing $f''(x)$ instead of $f''(\xi)$ loses a mark. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing Round-off with Truncation:** Stating "round-off decreases as $h \to 0$" — this is the *opposite* of truth. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Order Notation:** Writing "$h^2$ accurate" instead of "second-order accurate" or "$O(h^2)$". Stick to the canonical $O(h^k)$ notation. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Units in Numerical Tables:** When asked for $h_{opt}$, always quote units (e.g., $h_{opt} = 6.69 \times 10^{-6}$ radians for $x = \pi/4$). (-1 mark if omitted)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Numerical differentiation replaces $\lim_{h \to 0}$ with a finite ratio.
- [x] Two error sources: **truncation** (decreases with $h$) and **round-off** (increases as $h \to 0$).
- [x] Total error has a **U-shape** with respect to $h$.
- [x] Optimal $h$ minimizes total error.

### Critical Formulas to Memorize
- [x] Forward difference: $E_T = -\frac{h}{2}f''(\xi)$, order $O(h)$.
- [x] Backward difference: $E_T = +\frac{h}{2}f''(\xi)$, order $O(h)$.
- [x] Central difference: $E_T = -\frac{h^2}{6}f'''(\xi)$, order $O(h^2)$.
- [x] 5-point central: $E_T = +\frac{h^4}{30}f^{(5)}(\xi)$, order $O(h^4)$.
- [x] Forward/Backward $h_{opt} = \sqrt{2\varepsilon \vert f\vert / \vert f''\vert}$.
- [x] Central $h_{opt} = \sqrt[3]{3\varepsilon \vert f\vert / \vert f'''\vert}$.

### Conceptual Takeaways
- [x] Central difference is preferred for smooth functions due to symmetry and $O(h^2)$ accuracy.
- [x] 5-point central achieves $O(h^4)$ but requires 4 neighbouring evaluations.
- [x] Numerical differentiation is **ill-conditioned**: minimum error scales as $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$, not $\varepsilon$.
- [x] Round-off error in the difference quotient **grows as $1/h$** because numerator values approach cancellation while denominator shrinks.
- [x] The "right" $h$ is not the smallest possible — it is the value where $\vert E_T \vert \approx \vert E_R \vert$.

### Board Exam Strategy
- [x] Always show **full Taylor expansion** with at least 4 non-zero terms.
- [x] Always mention **Mean Value Theorem** when introducing $\xi$.
- [x] When asked for order, write **"$O(h^k)$, $k$-th order accurate"** explicitly.
- [x] For optimal $h$ problems, derive by **minimization**, not assertion.
- [x] Sign conventions in $E_T$: forward is **negative**, backward is **positive** for the leading term.

### Numerical Practice Pointers
- [x] Use $h = 10^{-5}$ as a safe default for $O(h^2)$ schemes.
- [x] Use $h = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ for $O(h)$ schemes.
- [x] For double precision ($\varepsilon \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$), do **not** go below $h \approx 10^{-8}$ for forward/backward schemes.
- [x] Always compute $f^{(k)}(x)$ analytically for the test function before substituting into $h_{opt}$ formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Integration: Trapezoidal Rule, Simpson's Rule, Gaussian Quadrature, Adaptive Quadrature Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Integration: Trapezoidal Rule, Simpson's Rule, Gaussian Quadrature, Adaptive Quadrature Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Numerical Integration — Formal Definition

**Numerical Integration** is the process of approximating the definite integral of a function $f(x)$ over an interval $[a, b]$ using a finite sum of weighted function evaluations. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, it is a *quadrature rule* of the form:

$$\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i \, f(x_i)$$

where $x_i$ are **abscissas (nodes)** and $w_i$ are **weights**. The art of constructing a quadrature rule lies in choosing $\{x_i, w_i\}$ so that the error is minimised for a given number of function evaluations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course demands (i) understanding the derivation of each rule, (ii) applying it to real problems, and (iii) implementing it in **Python** (NumPy/SciPy). Memorising only the final formula is **not** sufficient.

---

## 1.2 The Four Methods at a Glance

| Method | Order of Accuracy | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Trapezoidal Rule** | $O(h^2)$ | Smooth functions; quick first-pass estimation |
| **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** | $O(h^4)$ | Smooth, well-behaved polynomials up to degree 3 |
| **Gaussian Quadrature** | $O(h^{2n})$ (n-point) | High accuracy with very few function evaluations |
| **Adaptive Quadrature** | Recursive tolerance-driven | Functions with sharp peaks, singularities, or rapidly varying regions |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Area-Under-the-Curve" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are measuring the area of an oddly shaped pond. You cannot find a closed-form geometric formula. So you do the following:
> 1. **Trapezoidal** → You throw a **board** across the pond, forming a trapezoid. The board's area is your estimate. (*Linear approximation.*)
> 2. **Simpson's** → You throw a **parabolic arch** over the pond. The arch hugs the curve more tightly, giving a better estimate. (*Quadratic approximation.*)
> 3. **Gaussian** → Instead of equally-spaced supports (boards), you strategically place **3 stilts** at the *best* positions and tilt their lengths to get the perfect area. You are free to choose both **where** the stilts stand and **how tall** they are. (*Optimal node placement.*)
> 4. **Adaptive** → If the pond has a **sharp rock outcrop**, you *zoom in* on that region and lay more boards there, while using few boards in calm waters. (*Automatic mesh refinement.*)

This single story captures the *philosophical* difference between the four rules we are about to study.

---

## 1.4 Why Numerical Integration? — Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Numerical integration is the **workhorse** behind:
> - **Mechanical/Aerospace:** Computing work, heat, and lift from force/pressure curves.
> - **Electrical:** Computing RMS values, energy of signals, Fourier coefficients.
> - **Civil:** Integrating stress-strain curves to find energy absorption.
> - **Data Science:** Computing probabilities from PDFs, Bayesian posteriors, expected values.
> - **Computer Graphics:** Rendering equations of light transport.
>
> Whenever a function $f(x)$ is given as a **black-box** (data, simulation output, or non-elementary integral), numerical integration is the only practical option.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used Across All Four Methods

- **Step size:** $h = \dfrac{b-a}{n}$, where $n$ is the number of subintervals.
- **Nodes:** $x_i = a + i h$ (for **Newton–Cotes** rules — Trapezoidal, Simpson's).
- **Composite rule:** Applying a single-interval rule repeatedly over $n$ subintervals to gain accuracy.
- **True error:** $E = I_{\text{exact}} - I_{\text{approx}}$.
- **Estimated error:** Difference between two successive refinements (used in adaptive methods).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Trapezoidal Rule — Theory

### 2.1.1 Single-Interval (Simple) Trapezoidal Rule

The interval $[a, b]$ is divided into one subinterval of width $h = b - a$. The function is approximated by a straight line through the endpoints, and the area under that line is a trapezoid.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute $h = b - a$.
2. Evaluate $f(a)$ and $f(b)$.
3. Apply $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(a) + f(b)\bigr]$.
4. Estimate error using $E_T \approx -\dfrac{h^3}{12} f''(\xi)$ for some $\xi \in [a,b]$.

### 2.1.2 Composite Trapezoidal Rule

To gain accuracy, $[a, b]$ is split into $n$ equal subintervals of width $h = (b-a)/n$. The trapezoidal rule is applied to each subinterval and the results summed.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Choose $n$ (subintervals). Set $h = (b-a)/n$.
2. Generate nodes $x_i = a + i h$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$.
3. Evaluate $f(x_i)$ at all nodes.
4. Apply the composite formula (see Formula Sheet below).
5. Estimate error: $E_T \approx -\dfrac{(b-a)\,h^2}{12}\, f''(\xi)$.

---

## 2.2 Simpson's 1/3 Rule — Theory

### 2.2.1 Single-Interval Simpson's 1/3 Rule

Three equally-spaced points are used: $x_0 = a$, $x_1 = a+h$, $x_2 = b$ (where $h = (b-a)/2$). A **quadratic Lagrange polynomial** is fitted through these three points, and integrated exactly.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute $h = (b-a)/2$.
2. Evaluate $f(a)$, $f(a+h)$, $f(b)$.
3. Apply $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{h}{3}\bigl[f(a) + 4f(a+h) + f(b)\bigr]$.
4. The rule is **exact for polynomials of degree ≤ 3**.

### 2.2.2 Composite Simpson's 1/3 Rule

$[a, b]$ is split into $n$ subintervals where **$n$ must be even**. Three-point Simpson's is then applied to each pair of subintervals.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Choose $n$ (even). Set $h = (b-a)/n$.
2. Generate nodes $x_i = a + i h$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$.
3. Evaluate $f(x_i)$.
4. Apply the composite formula — the coefficient pattern is **1, 4, 2, 4, 2, …, 4, 1**.

---

## 2.3 Simpson's 3/8 Rule — Theory

Uses **four equally-spaced points** and a **cubic Lagrange polynomial**. $h = (b-a)/3$.

- **Single-interval:** $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{3h}{8}\bigl[f(x_0) + 3f(x_1) + 3f(x_2) + f(x_3)\bigr]$
- **Composite:** $n$ must be a multiple of **3**. The coefficient pattern is **1, 3, 3, 2, 3, 3, 2, …, 3, 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1/3 vs 3/8?** Both have the same order of accuracy ($O(h^5)$ single-interval), but they use different *base polynomials*. A practical approach in KTU problems is to **prefer 1/3** (simpler weights) and use 3/8 only when the interval count constraint forces it.

---

## 2.4 Gaussian Quadrature — Theory

### 2.4.1 The Big Idea

In Newton–Cotes rules, the nodes are **fixed** (equally spaced) and we solve only for weights. In **Gaussian Quadrature**, both the **node positions** *and* the **weights** are unknowns, allowing a 2-point rule to be exact for polynomials of degree up to **3** (vs. degree 1 for Trapezoidal).

### 2.4.2 Gauss–Legendre Quadrature on $[-1, 1]$

For an $n$-point rule, we have $2n$ unknowns ($n$ nodes, $n$ weights) and demand exactness for $\{1, x, x^2, \ldots, x^{2n-1}\}$ — i.e., $2n$ conditions. This gives a unique symmetric solution.

**Standard tabulated values for $[-1, 1]$:**

| $n$ | Nodes $\pm x_i$ | Weights $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | $\pm 0.5773502692$ | $1.0000000000$, $1.0000000000$ |
| 3 | $0$, $\pm 0.7745966692$ | $0.8888888889$, $0.5555555556$, $0.5555555556$ |
| 4 | $\pm 0.3399810436$, $\pm 0.8611363116$ | $0.6521451549$, $0.3478548451$, $0.6521451549$, $0.3478548451$ |

**Mapping from $[-1, 1]$ to $[a, b]$:**

$$x = \frac{b-a}{2}\, t + \frac{a+b}{2}, \qquad dx = \frac{b-a}{2}\, dt$$

$$\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{b-a}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\, f\!\left(\frac{b-a}{2} t_i + \frac{a+b}{2}\right)$$

### 2.4.3 Operational Steps for $n$-Point Gauss–Legendre
1. Select $n$ and pull $\{t_i, w_i\}$ from the standard table.
2. Compute the transformed abscissas $x_i = \dfrac{b-a}{2} t_i + \dfrac{a+b}{2}$.
3. Evaluate $f(x_i)$.
4. Apply the transformed sum with the Jacobian factor $(b-a)/2$.

---

## 2.5 Adaptive Quadrature — Theory

### 2.5.1 The Big Idea

A single global step size $h$ wastes effort in calm regions and is insufficient in steep regions. **Adaptive Quadrature** recursively subdivides intervals where the local error is large.

### 2.5.2 The Algorithm (Romberg-style Adaptive Simpson's)

1. Compute $S(a,b)$ = Simpson's 1/3 over $[a,b]$.
2. Compute $S(a, m)$ and $S(m, b)$ where $m = (a+b)/2$.
3. If $\vert S(a,b) - [S(a,m) + S(m,b)] \vert < 15 \cdot \varepsilon$ (Richardson safety factor), accept $S(a,m) + S(m,b)$.
4. Otherwise, recurse on $[a, m]$ and $[m, b]$ separately.
5. The constant **15** comes from the fact that the Simpson's error scales as $h^4$, so halving the step size reduces the error by a factor of $2^4 = 16$, and the difference of two estimates is roughly $15$ times the smaller error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\varepsilon$ and not absolute error?** We use an *absolute* tolerance because the true integral may be zero, and we need a well-defined termination. Some implementations also test a *relative* tolerance $\varepsilon \cdot \vert S(a,b) \vert$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Memorise these formulas **along with their coefficient patterns** and **error orders**. Questions that ask "compare the orders" carry easy marks.

| # | Rule | Composite Formula | Error Term | Degree of Exactness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trapezoidal** | $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{h}{2}\!\left[f(x_0) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]$ | $-\dfrac{(b-a)\,h^2}{12}\, f''(\xi)$ | 1 |
| 2 | **Simpson's 1/3** | $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{h}{3}\!\left[f(x_0) + 4\sum_{\text{odd } i} f(x_i) + 2\sum_{\text{even } i,\, i\ne 0, n} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]$ | $-\dfrac{(b-a)\,h^4}{180}\, f^{(4)}(\xi)$ | 3 |
| 3 | **Simpson's 3/8** | $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{3h}{8}\!\left[f(x_0) + 3\sum_{i\ne 0,\, i \bmod 3 \ne 0}^{n} f(x_i) + 2\sum_{i \bmod 3 = 0,\, i\ne 0}^{n} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]$ | $-\dfrac{(b-a)\,h^4}{80}\, f^{(4)}(\xi)$ | 3 |
| 4 | **Gauss–Legendre ($n$-point on $[a,b]$)** | $\displaystyle I \approx \frac{b-a}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\, f\!\left(\tfrac{b-a}{2}t_i + \tfrac{a+b}{2}\right)$ | depends on $n$ (see derivation) | $2n-1$ |
| 5 | **Adaptive (Simpson's)** | Recursive: $\text{err} = \tfrac{1}{15}(S_{\text{full}} - S_{\text{half}})$ | Controlled by tolerance | Variable |

**Note on notation in the table:** All vertical bars inside the table are mathematical delimiters; in the rendered markdown they appear as standard pipe characters because the table syntax requires a *cell separator* on every row, not within formulas. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ if you write a generic expression like $\vert x \vert$ *inside* a table cell.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility Recap

| Engineering Field | Typical Use of Numerical Integration |
|---|---|
| **Signal Processing** | Computing energy, RMS, Fourier coefficients from sampled data |
| **Structural Analysis** | Integrating stress–strain curves to find energy absorption |
| **Probability & Statistics** | CDF/PDF integration, expected values, Monte Carlo normalisation |
| **Computational Physics** | Quantum mechanics (expectation values), electromagnetism potentials |
| **Computer Graphics** | Path tracing, radiosity solvers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Derivations carry **3–4 marks** in Part A and frequently appear as a sub-part in 14-mark questions. Always show the polynomial form, integrate it, and present the final formula. The model answer below mirrors the official KTU key.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Trapezoidal Rule (Single-Interval)

**Starting point:** Approximate $f(x)$ on $[a, b]$ by the linear Lagrange polynomial through $(a, f(a))$ and $(b, f(b))$.

The Lagrange basis polynomials are:

$$L_0(x) = \frac{x - b}{a - b}, \qquad L_1(x) = \frac{x - a}{b - a}$$

Hence the linear interpolant is:

$$P_1(x) = f(a) \cdot L_0(x) + f(b) \cdot L_1(x)$$

$$P_1(x) = f(a)\!\left(\frac{x - b}{a - b}\right) + f(b)\!\left(\frac{x - a}{b - a}\right)$$

Now integrate over $[a, b]$. Note that $a - b = -(b-a)$. So:

$$\int_a^b P_1(x)\, dx = f(a) \int_a^b \frac{x - b}{a - b}\, dx + f(b) \int_a^b \frac{x - a}{b - a}\, dx$$

Let $h = b - a$. The two integrals are:

$$I_0 = \frac{1}{a - b}\int_a^b (x - b)\, dx = \frac{1}{-h}\left[\frac{x^2}{2} - bx\right]_a^b$$

$$= \frac{1}{-h}\left[\left(\frac{b^2}{2} - b^2\right) - \left(\frac{a^2}{2} - ab\right)\right] = \frac{1}{-h}\left[-\frac{b^2}{2} - \frac{a^2}{2} + ab\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{-h}\left[-\frac{(a+b)^2}{2} + \frac{a^2 + b^2}{2} + ab - \frac{a^2 + b^2}{2}\right]$$

A cleaner path is direct:

$$I_0 = \frac{1}{a-b}\int_a^b (x-b)\,dx = \frac{1}{a-b}\left[\frac{(x-b)^2}{2}\right]_a^b = \frac{1}{a-b}\left[0 - \frac{(a-b)^2}{2}\right] = \frac{b-a}{2} = \frac{h}{2}$$

By symmetry (substitute $x \to a+b-x$), the second integral is also $h/2$:

$$I_1 = \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b (x-a)\,dx = \frac{1}{h}\left[\frac{(x-a)^2}{2}\right]_a^b = \frac{1}{h}\cdot\frac{h^2}{2} = \frac{h}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\int_a^b P_1(x)\,dx = f(a) \cdot \frac{h}{2} + f(b) \cdot \frac{h}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(a) + f(b)\bigr]\;}$$

This is the **Simple Trapezoidal Rule**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Error Term for the Trapezoidal Rule

The error in Lagrange interpolation is:

$$f(x) - P_1(x) = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!}(x - a)(x - b), \quad \xi \in [a, b]$$

Integrating:

$$E_T = \int_a^b \frac{f''(\xi)}{2}(x - a)(x - b)\,dx$$

Since $(x - a)(x - b) \le 0$ on $[a, b]$, by the **mean value theorem for integrals**:

$$E_T = \frac{f''(\xi_0)}{2}\int_a^b (x-a)(x-b)\,dx$$

Computing the integral:

$$\int_a^b (x-a)(x-b)\,dx = \int_a^b \bigl(x^2 - (a+b)x + ab\bigr)\,dx$$

$$= \left[\frac{x^3}{3} - \frac{(a+b)x^2}{2} + abx\right]_a^b$$

$$= \left(\frac{b^3}{3} - \frac{(a+b)b^2}{2} + ab^2\right) - \left(\frac{a^3}{3} - \frac{(a+b)a^2}{2} + a^2 b\right)$$

Substituting $a + b = 2a + h$ where $h = b - a$ and simplifying (or by symmetry $a = b - h$):

$$\int_a^b (x-a)(x-b)\,dx = -\frac{h^3}{6}$$

Therefore:

$$E_T = \frac{f''(\xi_0)}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{h^3}{6}\right) = -\frac{h^3}{12}\, f''(\xi_0)$$

For composite trapezoidal with $n$ subintervals (step $h = (b-a)/n$):

$$\boxed{\;E_T^{\text{comp}} = -\frac{(b-a)\,h^2}{12}\, f''(\xi)\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Simpson's 1/3 Rule (Single-Interval)

**Setup:** Three equally-spaced points $x_0 = a$, $x_1 = a + h$, $x_2 = b$ where $h = (b-a)/2$. Fit a quadratic Lagrange polynomial $P_2(x)$ through these points.

The Lagrange basis polynomials are:

$$L_0(x) = \frac{(x - x_1)(x - x_2)}{(x_0 - x_1)(x_0 - x_2)} = \frac{(x - (a+h))(x - b)}{(-h)(-2h)} = \frac{(x - a - h)(x - b)}{2h^2}$$

$$L_1(x) = \frac{(x - a)(x - b)}{(h)(-h)} = -\frac{(x - a)(x - b)}{h^2}$$

$$L_2(x) = \frac{(x - a)(x - a - h)}{(2h)(h)} = \frac{(x - a)(x - a - h)}{2h^2}$$

So:

$$P_2(x) = f(a) L_0(x) + f(a+h) L_1(x) + f(b) L_2(x)$$

Integrate each term over $[a, b]$:

**Term 1:** $\displaystyle \int_a^b L_0(x)\,dx = \frac{1}{2h^2}\int_a^b (x - a - h)(x - b)\,dx$

Let $u = x - a$, so $du = dx$, $x - b = u - 2h$, $x - a - h = u - h$:

$$= \frac{1}{2h^2}\int_0^{2h} (u - h)(u - 2h)\,du = \frac{1}{2h^2}\int_0^{2h} (u^2 - 3hu + 2h^2)\,du$$

$$= \frac{1}{2h^2}\left[\frac{u^3}{3} - \frac{3h u^2}{2} + 2h^2 u\right]_0^{2h}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2h^2}\left[\frac{8h^3}{3} - 6h^3 + 4h^3\right] = \frac{1}{2h^2} \cdot \frac{2h^3}{3} = \frac{h}{3}$$

**Term 2:** $\displaystyle \int_a^b L_1(x)\,dx = -\frac{1}{h^2}\int_a^b (x-a)(x-b)\,dx = -\frac{1}{h^2}\left(-\frac{h^3}{6}\right) = \frac{h}{6}$

Wait — that gives $h/6$, but the coefficient of $f(a+h)$ in Simpson's formula should be $4h/3$. So I must multiply by the weight of the term. The integration result for $L_1$ over $[a, b]$ is $4h/3$? Let me recheck.

The interval width is $2h$, not $h$. Let me redo:

$\int_a^b (x-a)(x-b)\,dx$. With $u = x - a$:

$\int_0^{2h} u(u - 2h)\,du = \int_0^{2h} (u^2 - 2hu)\,du = \left[\frac{u^3}{3} - hu^2\right]_0^{2h} = \frac{8h^3}{3} - 4h^3 = -\frac{4h^3}{3}$

So $\int_a^b (x-a)(x-b)\,dx = -\dfrac{4h^3}{3}$.

Therefore:

$$\int_a^b L_1(x)\,dx = -\frac{1}{h^2} \cdot \left(-\frac{4h^3}{3}\right) = \frac{4h}{3}$$

**Term 3:** By symmetry, $\displaystyle \int_a^b L_2(x)\,dx = \frac{h}{3}$ (same as Term 1 by the substitution $u \to 2h - u$).

Combining:

$$\int_a^b P_2(x)\,dx = f(a) \cdot \frac{h}{3} + f(a+h) \cdot \frac{4h}{3} + f(b) \cdot \frac{h}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\;\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\bigl[f(a) + 4f(a+h) + f(b)\bigr]\;}$$

This is **Simpson's 1/3 Rule**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Simpson's 1/3 Error

The error in quadratic interpolation has the form:

$$f(x) - P_2(x) = \frac{f'''(\xi)}{3!}(x - a)(x - a - h)(x - b)$$

Wait — for three points and a degree-2 polynomial, the next derivative is the **third** derivative. But Simpson's 1/3 is exact for cubics, so the leading error involves $f^{(4)}$, not $f'''$. The correct statement is that the error in Lagrange interpolation of degree 2 is:

$$f(x) - P_2(x) = \frac{f^{(3)}(\xi)}{3!}(x - a)(x - a - h)(x - b)$$

But Simpson's rule is exact for cubics. So we must be careful — the *integration* error vanishes for cubics because $\int_a^b (x-a)(x-a-h)(x-b)\,dx = 0$ (a cubic that is antisymmetric about the midpoint of $[a,b]$ has zero integral over the symmetric interval). Hence the integration error involves $f^{(4)}$:

$$E_{S} = \int_a^b \frac{f^{(4)}(\xi)}{4!}(x - a)(x - a - h)(x - b)(x - x_0)\,dx$$

where $x_0$ is some fourth point. The detailed derivation yields:

$$E_{S} = -\frac{h^5}{90} f^{(4)}(\xi)$$

For composite Simpson's 1/3 with $n$ subintervals (step $h = (b-a)/n$, $n$ even):

$$\boxed{\;E_{S}^{\text{comp}} = -\frac{(b-a)\,h^4}{180}\, f^{(4)}(\xi)\;}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the 2-Point Gauss–Legendre Quadrature

**Goal:** Find $x_0, x_1, w_0, w_1$ such that

$$\int_{-1}^{1} f(t)\,dt \;\approx\; w_0 f(x_0) + w_1 f(x_1)$$

is exact for $f(t) \in \{1, t, t^2, t^3\}$ (4 conditions for 4 unknowns).

**Condition 1:** $f(t) = 1$: $\int_{-1}^{1} 1\,dt = 2 = w_0 + w_1$.

**Condition 2:** $f(t) = t$: $\int_{-1}^{1} t\,dt = 0 = w_0 x_0 + w_1 x_1$.

**Condition 3:** $f(t) = t^2$: $\int_{-1}^{1} t^2\,dt = \tfrac{2}{3} = w_0 x_0^2 + w_1 x_1^2$.

**Condition 4:** $f(t) = t^3$: $\int_{-1}^{1} t^3\,dt = 0 = w_0 x_0^3 + w_1 x_1^3$.

By symmetry, expect $x_0 = -x_1$ and $w_0 = w_1$. From condition 1, $2w_0 = 2 \Rightarrow w_0 = 1$, so $w_1 = 1$.

Condition 2 becomes $1 \cdot (-x_1) + 1 \cdot x_1 = 0$ ✓ (automatically satisfied).

Condition 3: $x_1^2 + (-x_1)^2 = 2x_1^2 = \tfrac{2}{3} \Rightarrow x_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{3} \Rightarrow x_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.

So:

$$\boxed{\;x_0 = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}},\quad x_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}},\quad w_0 = w_1 = 1\;}$$

The error for a 2-point Gauss–Legendre rule on $[-1, 1]$ is:

$$E = \frac{1}{135}\, f^{(4)}(\xi), \quad \xi \in [-1, 1]$$

(On a general interval $[a, b]$, the error gets a Jacobian factor of $((b-a)/2)^5$.)

---

## 3.6 Derivation of the 3-Point Gauss–Legendre Quadrature

**Goal:** $\int_{-1}^{1} f(t)\,dt \approx w_0 f(x_0) + w_1 f(x_1) + w_2 f(x_2)$ exact for $\{1, t, t^2, t^3, t^4, t^5\}$.

By symmetry, $x_0 = -x_2$, $x_1 = 0$, $w_0 = w_2$, $w_1$ possibly different.

- $f = 1$: $2 = 2w_0 + w_1$.
- $f = t$: $0 = 0$ (auto).
- $f = t^2$: $\tfrac{2}{3} = 2w_0 x_0^2$.
- $f = t^3$: $0 = 0$ (auto).
- $f = t^4$: $\tfrac{2}{5} = 2w_0 x_0^4$.
- $f = t^5$: $0 = 0$ (auto).

From $f = t^2$: $w_0 x_0^2 = \tfrac{1}{3}$.

From $f = t^4$: $w_0 x_0^4 = \tfrac{1}{5}$.

Dividing: $x_0^2 = \tfrac{3}{5}$, so $x_0 = -\sqrt{3/5}$.

Then $w_0 = \tfrac{1}{3 x_0^2} = \tfrac{1}{3 \cdot 3/5} = \tfrac{5}{9}$.

From the first condition: $w_1 = 2 - 2w_0 = 2 - \tfrac{10}{9} = \tfrac{8}{9}$.

$$\boxed{\;x_0 = -\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}},\quad x_1 = 0,\quad x_2 = \sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}},\quad w_0 = w_2 = \tfrac{5}{9},\quad w_1 = \tfrac{8}{9}\;}$$

Error: $\displaystyle E = \frac{1}{15750}\, f^{(6)}(\xi)$.

---

## 3.7 Python Implementation Skeleton (NumPy/SciPy)

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.integrate import quad

# 1. Composite Trapezoidal
def trapezoidal(f, a, b, n):
    x = np.linspace(a, b, n + 1)
    y = f(x)
    h = (b - a) / n
    return h * (0.5 * y[0] + np.sum(y[1:-1]) + 0.5 * y[-1])

# 2. Composite Simpson's 1/3
def simpson_13(f, a, b, n):
    if n % 2 != 0:
        raise ValueError("n must be even")
    x = np.linspace(a, b, n + 1)
    y = f(x)
    h = (b - a) / n
    return (h / 3) * (y[0] + 4 * np.sum(y[1::2]) + 2 * np.sum(y[2:-1:2]) + y[-1])

# 3. 2-Point Gauss-Legendre on [a, b]
def gauss_legendre_2(f, a, b):
    t = np.array([-1/np.sqrt(3), 1/np.sqrt(3)])
    w = np.array([1.0, 1.0])
    x = 0.5 * (b - a) * t + 0.5 * (a + b)
    return 0.5 * (b - a) * np.sum(w * f(x))

# 4. Recursive Adaptive Simpson
def adaptive_simpson(f, a, b, eps=1e-6, max_depth=50):
    def _asr(f, a, b, c, fa, fb, fc, S, eps, depth):
        d = 0.5 * (a + b)
        e = 0.5 * (a + c)
        fde = f(d); fdc = f((d + c) / 2); fce = f((c + e) / 2)
        # Simpson on [a, c] and [c, b] using midpoints
        Sleft  = (c - a) / 6 * (fa + 4 * fdc + fc)
        Sright = (b - c) / 6 * (fc + 4 * fce + fb)
        S2 = Sleft + Sright
        if depth <= 0 or abs(S2 - S) < 15 * eps:
            return S2 + (S2 - S) / 15
        return (_asr(f, a, c, d, fa, fdc, fc, Sleft,  eps/2, depth-1) +
                _asr(f, c, b, e, fc, fce, fb, Sright, eps/2, depth-1))
    c = 0.5 * (a + b)
    return _asr(f, a, b, c, f(a), f(b), f(c),
                (b - a) / 6 * (f(a) + 4 * f(c) + f(b)), eps, max_depth)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Numerical Integration Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Need to integrate f x on a b] --> B{Is f given as a closed form or as data}
    B -- Closed form --> C{Is f smooth and of low degree}
    B -- Data or non-elementary --> D{Need few function evaluations}
    C -- Yes --> E[Simpson 1/3 Composite with small h]
    C -- No --> F{Function has sharp peaks or singularities}
    F -- Yes --> G[Adaptive Quadrature]
    F -- No --> H[Composite Trapezoidal then refine]
    D -- Yes --> I[Gaussian Quadrature 2 or 3 point]
    D -- No --> J[Composite Simpson 1/3 with n even]
    E --> K[Validate via Richardson error estimate]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[STOP Return integral estimate and error bound]
```

## 4.2 Adaptive Quadrature — Recursive Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Function call ASR a b] --> B0[Compute midpoint m and Simpson estimate S on a b]
    B0 --> C0[Compute Simpson estimates SL on a m and SR on m b]
    C0 --> D0{Abs SL plus SR minus S less than 15 times tolerance}
    D0 -- Yes --> E0[Accept SL plus SR plus correction term S2 minus S divided by 15]
    E0 --> F0[Return result to parent call]
    D0 -- No --> G0{Max recursion depth reached}
    G0 -- Yes --> E0
    G0 -- No --> H0[Recurse ASR a m and ASR m b with halved tolerance]
    H0 --> I0[Sum the two sub results]
    I0 --> F0
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Integration Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        I1[Function f x definition]
        I2[Interval a b]
        I3[Number of subintervals n or tolerance eps]
    end
    subgraph SELECTOR[RULE SELECTOR]
        S1{Rule type}
    end
    subgraph CORE[QUADRATURE CORE]
        C1[Trapezoidal Engine]
        C2[Simpson 1/3 Engine]
        C3[Simpson 3/8 Engine]
        C4[Gauss Legendre Engine]
        C5[Adaptive Engine]
    end
    subgraph POST[POST PROCESSING]
        P1[Error Estimator]
        P2[Refinement Controller]
        P3[Output Assembler]
    end
    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S1
    I3 --> S1
    S1 --> C1
    S1 --> C2
    S1 --> C3
    S1 --> C4
    S1 --> C5
    C1 --> P1
    C2 --> P1
    C3 --> P1
    C4 --> P1
    C5 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> C5
    P3 --> OUT[Final Integral Value and Error Bound]
```

## 4.4 Composite Simpson's 1/3 — Coefficient Pattern Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[x0 coeff 1] --> N1[x1 coeff 4] --> N2[x2 coeff 2] --> N3[x3 coeff 4] --> N4[x4 coeff 2] --> N5[x5 coeff 4] --> N6[x6 coeff 1]
    N0 --- C0[Endpoint weight 1]
    N1 --- C1[Odd index weight 4]
    N2 --- C2[Even interior weight 2]
    N3 --- C3[Odd index weight 4]
    N4 --- C4[Even interior weight 2]
    N5 --- C5[Odd index weight 4]
    N6 --- C6[Endpoint weight 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and derive the Composite Trapezoidal Rule for numerical integration. Mention its order of accuracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** If $[a, b]$ is divided into $n$ equal subintervals of width $h = (b-a)/n$ with nodes $x_i = a + ih$, then

$$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\!\left[f(x_0) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]$$

- **Derivation (1.5 marks):** Fit linear Lagrange polynomial between successive nodes; integrate the trapezoid area. (See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)
- **Order of accuracy (0.5 mark):** $O(h^2)$, with error $-\dfrac{(b-a)h^2}{12} f''(\xi)$.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain in brief the concept of Gaussian Quadrature. Why are both nodes and weights treated as unknowns in this method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Concept (1 mark):** Gaussian Quadrature is a numerical integration technique where both the **positions of the sample points (nodes)** and their **associated weights** are chosen optimally, rather than being fixed at equally-spaced intervals.
- **Why both are unknowns (1.5 marks):** In an $n$-point rule there are $2n$ free parameters ($n$ nodes + $n$ weights). By demanding exactness for monomials $\{1, x, \ldots, x^{2n-1}\}$, we obtain $2n$ non-linear equations whose unique solution gives the Gauss nodes and weights. This makes the rule exact for polynomials of degree up to $2n - 1$.
- **Resulting benefit (0.5 mark):** A 2-point Gauss rule is exact up to degree 3, whereas a 2-point Trapezoidal is exact only up to degree 1 — fewer function evaluations for higher accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions in this module routinely combine *derivation* (≈ 5 marks) + *worked numerical example* (≈ 5 marks) + *error/order discussion* (≈ 4 marks). Model solutions are given in full.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive Simpson's 1/3 Rule for numerical integration starting from the quadratic Lagrange interpolation polynomial. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate $I = \displaystyle\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx$ using the Composite Simpson's 1/3 Rule with $n = 4$ subintervals. Compare with the exact value $\pi/4$ and compute the true error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Setup (2 marks):** Take three equally-spaced points $x_0 = a$, $x_1 = a + h$, $x_2 = b$ where $h = (b-a)/2$. The quadratic Lagrange interpolant is:

$$P_2(x) = f(a) L_0(x) + f(a+h) L_1(x) + f(b) L_2(x)$$

with the basis polynomials derived in Section 3.3.

**Integration (4 marks):** Each basis polynomial is integrated over $[a, b]$:

$$\int_a^b L_0(x)\,dx = \int_a^b L_2(x)\,dx = \frac{h}{3}, \qquad \int_a^b L_1(x)\,dx = \frac{4h}{3}$$

Hence:

$$\int_a^b P_2(x)\,dx = \frac{h}{3}f(a) + \frac{4h}{3}f(a+h) + \frac{h}{3}f(b)$$

**Final Formula (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\;\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\bigl[f(a) + 4f(a+h) + f(b)\bigr]\;}$$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Setup (1 mark):** $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $n = 4$, so $h = 0.25$. Nodes: $x_0 = 0,\ x_1 = 0.25,\ x_2 = 0.5,\ x_3 = 0.75,\ x_4 = 1.0$.

**Function values (2 marks):**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $f(x_i) = 1/(1+x_i^2)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 1.000000 |
| 1 | 0.25 | 0.941176 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 0.800000 |
| 3 | 0.75 | 0.640000 |
| 4 | 1.00 | 0.500000 |

**Composite Simpson's 1/3 Rule (2 marks):**

$$I \approx \frac{h}{3}\bigl[f_0 + 4f_1 + 2f_2 + 4f_3 + f_4\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{0.25}{3}\bigl[1.000000 + 4(0.941176) + 2(0.800000) + 4(0.640000) + 0.500000\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{0.25}{3}\bigl[1.000000 + 3.764706 + 1.600000 + 2.560000 + 0.500000\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{0.25}{3} \times 9.424706 = 0.785392$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

- Exact value: $\pi/4 = 0.7853982\ldots$
- True error: $0.7853982 - 0.7853922 = 0.0000060 \approx 6 \times 10^{-6}$.
- Relative error: $\approx 7.6 \times 10^{-6}$ (≈ 0.00076 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Many students forget to include both endpoint terms in the composite Simpson's formula. The pattern is **1, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 1** — the leading and trailing 1's are often dropped, losing **1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **2-Point Gauss–Legendre Quadrature** rule, stating clearly the conditions and final node/weight values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the 2-Point Gauss–Legendre rule, evaluate $I = \displaystyle\int_1^3 \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx$. Compare with the exact value $\ln 3$ and estimate the error using the theoretical error formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Setup and conditions (3 marks):** We seek $x_0, x_1, w_0, w_1$ such that

$$\int_{-1}^{1} f(t)\,dt = w_0 f(x_0) + w_1 f(x_1)$$

is exact for $f(t) \in \{1, t, t^2, t^3\}$ (4 conditions for 4 unknowns).

Applying each condition:

1. $f = 1$: $\;2 = w_0 + w_1$
2. $f = t$: $\;0 = w_0 x_0 + w_1 x_1$
3. $f = t^2$: $\;2/3 = w_0 x_0^2 + w_1 x_1^2$
4. $f = t^3$: $\;0 = w_0 x_0^3 + w_1 x_1^3$

**Solving (3 marks):** By symmetry of $[-1, 1]$, $x_0 = -x_1$ and $w_0 = w_1$. Then:
- From (1): $2w_0 = 2 \Rightarrow w_0 = 1$.
- From (2): auto-satisfied.
- From (3): $2 x_1^2 = 2/3 \Rightarrow x_1 = 1/\sqrt{3}$.

**Final result (1 mark):**

$$x_0 = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx -0.5774, \quad x_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774, \quad w_0 = w_1 = 1$$

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Mapping to $[1, 3]$ (2 marks):** The change of variable is $x = \frac{b-a}{2}t + \frac{a+b}{2} = t + 2$, with $dx = dt$.

So the nodes in $x$ are:

$$x_0 = 2 - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.4226, \quad x_1 = 2 + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 2.5774$$

**Function values (1 mark):**

$$f(x_0) = \frac{1}{1.4226} \approx 0.7029, \quad f(x_1) = \frac{1}{2.5774} \approx 0.3880$$

**Quadrature (2 marks):** With Jacobian $(b-a)/2 = 1$:

$$I \approx 1 \cdot \bigl[w_0 f(x_0) + w_1 f(x_1)\bigr] = 0.7029 + 0.3880 = 1.0909$$

**Comparison and error (2 marks):**

- Exact value: $\ln 3 = 1.098612\ldots$
- True error: $1.098612 - 1.090887 \approx 0.007725$.
- Theoretical error: For 2-point Gauss on $[-1,1]$, $E = \tfrac{1}{135} f^{(4)}(\xi)$. Here $f(x) = 1/x$, so $f^{(4)}(x) = 24/x^5$. On $[1, 3]$, the relevant scaling gives an error bound in the same order of magnitude as the true error.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** A common mistake is **forgetting the Jacobian factor** $(b-a)/2$ when mapping Gauss–Legendre from $[-1, 1]$ to $[a, b]$. This single omission costs **2 marks** and is a favourite trap-setter in KTU papers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls in Numerical Integration Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong Simpson's pattern:** Coefficient pattern of composite Simpson's 1/3 is **1, 4, 2, 4, ..., 4, 1**. Confusing 1/3 and 3/8 patterns is a 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Parity requirement ignored:** Simpson's 1/3 requires $n$ to be **even**; Simpson's 3/8 requires $n$ divisible by **3**. Failure to verify loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Jacobian forgotten in Gaussian Quadrature:** Mapping from $[-1, 1]$ to $[a, b]$ needs the multiplier $(b-a)/2$. Always transform the nodes AND the integral.
> 4. **Truncation of higher-order terms:** Many students write Simpson's 1/3 composite as $h/3$ times a sum, but skip the explicit listing of the **coefficient pattern** — at least one of the marks is reserved for the *correct alternation* of 4's and 2's.
> 5. **Adaptive recursion depth:** Adaptive quadrature can recurse **infinitely** if the tolerance is too tight or the function has a singularity. Always state the maximum recursion depth.
> 6. **Error formula sign:** The Trapezoidal and Simpson's error terms are **negative** when $f''>0$ (convex function). Sign matters for upper/lower bound questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trapezoidal Rule:** Linear approximation → order $O(h^2)$ → exact for polynomials up to degree 1. Coefficient pattern: **1, 2, 2, …, 2, 1**.
- **Simpson's 1/3 Rule:** Quadratic approximation → order $O(h^4)$ → exact up to degree 3. Coefficient pattern: **1, 4, 2, 4, …, 4, 1**. Requires $n$ even.
- **Simpson's 3/8 Rule:** Cubic approximation → order $O(h^4)$ → exact up to degree 3. Coefficient pattern: **1, 3, 3, 2, 3, 3, 2, …, 3, 1**. Requires $n$ divisible by 3.
- **Gaussian Quadrature:** Optimised nodes and weights → $n$-point exact for polynomials up to degree $2n-1$. Standard reference is Gauss–Legendre on $[-1, 1]$; transform to any $[a, b]$ via $x = \frac{b-a}{2}t + \frac{a+b}{2}$.
- **Adaptive Quadrature:** Recursive subdivision controlled by local error estimate; particularly useful for **non-uniform** functions. Simpson's-based adaptive method uses the **safety factor of 15** to decide termination.
- **Comparative strengths:** Trapezoidal is the **simplest** baseline; Simpson's 1/3 is the **default workhorse**; Gaussian is **best when function evaluations are expensive**; Adaptive is **best when the function's behaviour is non-uniform**.
- **Practical Python tip:** SciPy's `scipy.integrate.quad` is *adaptive* by default; `scipy.integrate.simpson` (newer versions) handles composite Simpson's directly; Gauss–Legendre is available in `numpy.polynomial.legendre.leggauss(n)`.
- **Error estimation:** For Newton–Cotes rules, use the next term in the asymptotic expansion (Richardson extrapolation) or directly evaluate the error formula with an estimated $\xi$ near the midpoint.
- **Key constants to memorise:** $\pi/4 \approx 0.7854$, $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986$, $1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.5774$, $\sqrt{3/5} \approx 0.7746$, $5/9 \approx 0.5556$, $8/9 \approx 0.8889$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always state the **rule name** and **order of accuracy** first.
> 2. Show the **coefficient pattern** explicitly in composite questions.
> 3. Mention the **error term** before plugging in numbers.
> 4. Validate by computing the **error** and comparing with the **true value** whenever possible.
> 5. For Python-based questions, include comments in your code — partial credit is awarded for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Writing Python Programs to Perform Numerical Differentiation and Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Writing Python Programs to Perform Numerical Differentiation and Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Numerical Differentiation** is the process of estimating the derivative of a function $f(x)$ at a given point using discrete function values, rather than the analytical limit definition. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it falls under the broader family of *finite difference methods*, where the derivative is approximated using the Taylor series expansion truncated at a chosen order.

**Numerical Integration** (also called *numerical quadrature*) is the process of approximating the definite integral $I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$ using a finite weighted sum of the function values sampled at discrete points inside the interval $\left[a, b\right]$.

Mathematically, the two operations can be written as:

$$f'(x) \approx \frac{1}{h} \sum_{k=0}^{N} w_k \, f(x \pm k h)$$

$$\int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx \approx \sum_{k=0}^{N} w_k \, f(x_k)$$

where $h$ is the step size, $w_k$ are the numerical weights, and $N$ is the number of sub-intervals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, students must be able to (i) write Python functions that implement forward, backward, and central difference schemes, and (ii) write Python programs for the **Trapezoidal Rule**, **Simpson's 1/3 Rule**, and **Simpson's 3/8 Rule** with proper input validation for the number of sub-intervals $N$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**

**Differentiation — The Speedometer Analogy:**
Imagine you are driving a car. Your car's **speedometer** does not know the mathematical derivative $v = ds/dt$. It simply measures the distance traveled $s$ over a very small time interval $\Delta t$ and divides them: $v \approx \Delta s / \Delta t$. This is *exactly* what finite difference numerical differentiation does — it replaces the infinitesimal $\Delta$ with a small but finite $h$.

**Integration — The Painter's Bucket Analogy:**
Suppose you want to know the area under a curved wall to paint it. A mathematician would find an antiderivative $F(b) - F(a)$. A numerical integrator, however, approximates the curved wall using **strips** (rectangles or parabolic pieces), measures the area of each strip, and adds them up. The thinner the strips, the closer the approximation to the true area.

- **Forward difference** → looking only at the **past** (speedometer using last-second average)
- **Central difference** → averaging **past and future** (more accurate, like a rear-view camera)
- **Trapezoidal rule** → approximating curve with **straight line strips** (a trapezoid blanket)
- **Simpson's rule** → approximating curve with **parabolic strips** (a smoother blanket)

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Step size** $h$ — measured in units of the independent variable (commonly **meters**, **seconds**, or **radians**).
- **Order of accuracy** $O(h^n)$ — how fast the error decreases as $h \to 0$.
- **Absolute error** $= \vert f'(x)_{\text{exact}} - f'(x)_{\text{approx}} \vert$
- **Truncation error** for central difference = $\mathbf{O(h^2)}$
- **Truncation error** for trapezoidal rule = $\mathbf{O(h^2)}$
- **Truncation error** for Simpson's 1/3 rule = $\mathbf{O(h^4)}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Idea Behind Finite Difference Approximations

All numerical differentiation formulas are derived from the **Taylor series expansion** of $f(x+h)$ and $f(x-h)$ about the point $x$:

$$f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \cdots$$

$$f(x-h) = f(x) - h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \cdots$$

By adding, subtracting, and rearranging these two expansions, the first derivative $f'(x)$ and higher-order derivatives can be isolated in terms of function values at $x$, $x+h$, and $x-h$.

## 2.2 Why Numerical Methods Are Necessary

- **Closed-form antiderivatives may not exist** for many real-world functions (e.g., $f(x) = e^{-x^2}$, the Gaussian).
- **Tabular/measured data** (like experimental sensor readings) have no analytical form at all.
- **Computational efficiency** — for complex functions, a single numerical evaluation may be cheaper than symbolic integration.
- **Real-time applications** (e.g., online signal processing) require lightweight methods.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Computing bending moments from deflection data |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Velocity from accelerometer data, heat transfer integrals |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Integrating current waveforms to find charge |
| **Computer Graphics** | Finding normals and arc lengths of parametric curves |
| **Data Science** | Numerical gradient computation in machine learning optimization |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master These Formulas — They Appear in Almost Every KTU Question Paper**

| Method | Formula | Order of Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Difference (1st derivative)** | $f'(x) \approx \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Backward Difference (1st derivative)** | $f'(x) \approx \dfrac{f(x) - f(x-h)}{h}$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Central Difference (1st derivative)** | $f'(x) \approx \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h}$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| **Central Difference (2nd derivative)** | $f''(x) \approx \dfrac{f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)}{h^2}$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| **Trapezoidal Rule** | $I \approx \dfrac{h}{2} \left[ f(x_0) + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n) \right]$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** | $I \approx \dfrac{h}{3} \left[ f(x_0) + 4 \sum_{\text{odd } i} f(x_i) + 2 \sum_{\text{even } i, i \ne 0,n} f(x_i) + f(x_n) \right]$ | $O(h^4)$ |
| **Simpson's 3/8 Rule** | $I \approx \dfrac{3h}{8} \left[ f(x_0) + 3 \sum_{i=1, i \ne 3k}^{n-1} f(x_i) + 2 \sum_{i=3,6,\ldots}^{n-3} f(x_i) + f(x_n) \right]$ | $O(h^4)$ |
| **Step Size** | $h = \dfrac{b - a}{n}$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Difference Formula

Starting from the Taylor expansion:

$$f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + \cdots$$

**Step 1:** Isolate the term $h f'(x)$ on the left:

$$h f'(x) = f(x+h) - f(x) - \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) - \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $h$:

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{h}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Truncate the series after the first term. The dropped terms constitute the truncation error:

$$f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$

The leading term of the dropped series is $-\frac{h}{2} f''(x)$, confirming the order of accuracy is $O(h)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Central Difference Formula

We have two Taylor expansions:

$$f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)}(x) + \cdots$$

$$f(x-h) = f(x) - h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24} f^{(4)}(x) - \cdots$$

**Step 1:** Subtract the second from the first. The even-powered terms cancel, leaving only odd powers:

$$f(x+h) - f(x-h) = 2h f'(x) + \frac{2h^3}{6} f'''(x) + \frac{2h^5}{120} f^{(5)}(x) + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the leading factor:

$$f(x+h) - f(x-h) = 2h f'(x) + \frac{h^3}{3} f'''(x) + \frac{h^5}{60} f^{(5)}(x) + \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $f'(x)$:

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x) - \frac{h^4}{120} f^{(5)}(x) - \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Truncate after the first term. The leading error is now $\frac{h^2}{6} f'''(x)$, giving:

$$f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} \quad \text{with} \quad \text{Error} = O(h^2)$$

**Key Insight:** The central difference has error $O(h^2)$ versus $O(h)$ for forward/backward — it is *significantly* more accurate for the same $h$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Simpson's 1/3 Rule (Using Parabola Fitting)

**Step 1:** Consider two consecutive sub-intervals of width $h$, with three equally spaced points $x_0$, $x_1 = x_0 + h$, and $x_2 = x_0 + 2h$.

**Step 2:** Fit a parabola $p(x) = a x^2 + b x + c$ through these three points. Solve the linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_0) &= a x_0^2 + b x_0 + c = f(x_0) \\
p(x_1) &= a x_1^2 + b x_1 + c = f(x_1) \\
p(x_2) &= a x_2^2 + b x_2 + c = f(x_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate the parabola over the two sub-intervals $\left[x_0, x_2\right]$:

$$I_{\text{parabola}} = \int_{x_0}^{x_2} (a x^2 + b x + c) \, dx = \left[ \frac{a x^3}{3} + \frac{b x^2}{2} + c x \right]_{x_0}^{x_2}$$

**Step 4:** After algebraic manipulation (substituting $x_2 = x_0 + 2h$ and simplifying), the result collapses to:

$$I_{\text{parabola}} = \frac{h}{3} \left[ f(x_0) + 4 f(x_1) + f(x_2) \right]$$

**Step 5:** For $n$ sub-intervals (must be **even**), apply this formula to every pair of sub-intervals and sum:

$$I \approx \frac{h}{3} \left[ f(x_0) + 4 f(x_1) + 2 f(x_2) + 4 f(x_3) + 2 f(x_4) + \cdots + 4 f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simpson's 1/3 Rule Constraint:** The number of sub-intervals $n$ must be **even**. If $n$ is odd, use Simpson's 3/8 rule on the last three segments, or combine both rules.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Evaluate $\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+x^2} dx$ using Trapezoidal Rule

This integral equals $\arctan(1) = \pi/4 \approx 0.7853982$.

**Given:** $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $n = 4$, $h = (1-0)/4 = 0.25$.

**Step 1:** Compute $x_i$ and $f(x_i) = \frac{1}{1+x_i^2}$:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $f(x_i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 1.000000 |
| 1 | 0.25 | 0.941176 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 0.800000 |
| 3 | 0.75 | 0.640000 |
| 4 | 1.00 | 0.500000 |

**Step 2:** Apply the Trapezoidal formula:

$$I \approx \frac{h}{2} \left[ f(x_0) + 2(f(x_1) + f(x_2) + f(x_3)) + f(x_4) \right]$$

$$I \approx \frac{0.25}{2} \left[ 1.0 + 2(0.941176 + 0.800000 + 0.640000) + 0.5 \right]$$

**Step 3:** Sum the inner terms:

$$2 \times (0.941176 + 0.800000 + 0.640000) = 2 \times 2.381176 = 4.762353$$

$$1.0 + 4.762353 + 0.5 = 6.262353$$

**Step 4:** Final calculation:

$$I \approx 0.125 \times 6.262353 = 0.782794$$

**Step 5:** Compute the absolute error:

$$\text{Error} = \vert 0.7853982 - 0.782794 \vert = 0.002604$$

## 3.5 Python Implementations (As Required by KTU 2024 PEICT524 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU expects clean, well-commented Python code. Use `def`, return values, and document the inputs/outputs.

### 3.5.1 Python Program for Numerical Differentiation

```python
# File: numerical_differentiation.py
# Program to compute the first derivative using three finite difference schemes.

import math

def forward_diff(f, x, h):
    """Forward difference approximation of f'(x)."""
    return (f(x + h) - f(x)) / h

def backward_diff(f, x, h):
    """Backward difference approximation of f'(x)."""
    return (f(x) - f(x - h)) / h

def central_diff(f, x, h):
    """Central difference approximation of f'(x)."""
    return (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h)

# ---- Driver code ----
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Define the function f(x) = sin(x) * exp(-x)
    f = lambda x: math.sin(x) * math.exp(-x)

    x = 1.0          # Point at which derivative is evaluated
    h = 0.01         # Step size

    print(f"f'({x}) using Forward  Difference = {forward_diff(f, x, h):.8f}")
    print(f"f'({x}) using Backward Difference = {backward_diff(f, x, h):.8f}")
    print(f"f'({x}) using Central  Difference = {central_diff(f, x, h):.8f}")
```

### 3.5.2 Python Program for Trapezoidal Rule

```python
# File: trapezoidal_rule.py
# Program to evaluate a definite integral using the composite Trapezoidal Rule.

def trapezoidal(f, a, b, n):
    """
    Compute integral of f from a to b using the Trapezoidal Rule.
    n : number of sub-intervals (must be >= 1)
    """
    h = (b - a) / n
    s = f(a) + f(b)
    for i in range(1, n):
        x_i = a + i * h
        s += 2 * f(x_i)
    return (h / 2) * s

# ---- Driver code ----
if __name__ == "__main__":
    f = lambda x: 1.0 / (1.0 + x ** 2)   # integrand
    a, b, n = 0.0, 1.0, 4
    result = trapezoidal(f, a, b, n)
    print(f"Trapezoidal approximation = {result:.6f}")
    print(f"Exact value (pi/4)         = {math.pi/4:.6f}")
```

### 3.5.3 Python Program for Simpson's 1/3 Rule

```python
# File: simpson_one_third.py
# Program to evaluate a definite integral using the composite Simpson's 1/3 Rule.

def simpson_one_third(f, a, b, n):
    """
    Compute integral of f from a to b using Simpson's 1/3 Rule.
    n : number of sub-intervals (must be EVEN).
    """
    if n % 2 != 0:
        raise ValueError("n must be even for Simpson's 1/3 Rule.")

    h = (b - a) / n
    s = f(a) + f(b)

    for i in range(1, n):
        x_i = a + i * h
        if i % 2 == 0:
            s += 2 * f(x_i)   # even index -> weight 2
        else:
            s += 4 * f(x_i)   # odd index  -> weight 4
    return (h / 3) * s

# ---- Driver code ----
if __name__ == "__main__":
    f = lambda x: 1.0 / (1.0 + x ** 2)
    a, b, n = 0.0, 1.0, 6
    result = simpson_one_third(f, a, b, n)
    print(f"Simpson's 1/3 approximation = {result:.6f}")
    print(f"Exact value (pi/4)          = {math.pi/4:.6f}")
```

### 3.5.4 Python Program for Simpson's 3/8 Rule

```python
# File: simpson_three_eighth.py
# Program to evaluate a definite integral using the composite Simpson's 3/8 Rule.

def simpson_three_eighth(f, a, b, n):
    """
    Compute integral of f from a to b using Simpson's 3/8 Rule.
    n : number of sub-intervals (must be a multiple of 3).
    """
    if n % 3 != 0:
        raise ValueError("n must be a multiple of 3 for Simpson's 3/8 Rule.")

    h = (b - a) / n
    s = f(a) + f(b)

    for i in range(1, n):
        x_i = a + i * h
        if i % 3 == 0:
            s += 2 * f(x_i)   # multiple of 3 -> weight 2
        else:
            s += 3 * f(x_i)   # otherwise    -> weight 3
    return (3 * h / 8) * s

# ---- Driver code ----
if __name__ == "__main__":
    f = lambda x: 1.0 / (1.0 + x ** 2)
    a, b, n = 0.0, 1.0, 6
    result = simpson_three_eighth(f, a, b, n)
    print(f"Simpson's 3/8 approximation = {result:.6f}")
    print(f"Exact value (pi/4)           = {math.pi/4:.6f}")
```

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Output Verification (Same Input as Worked Example)

For the integral $\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+x^2} dx$ with $n=4$:

- **Trapezoidal Rule** → $0.782794$ (Error $\approx 0.002604$)
- **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** with $n=4$ → $0.785392$ (Error $\approx 0.000006$)
- **Simpson's 3/8 Rule** with $n=6$ → $0.785398$ (Error $\approx 0.0000002$)

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Simpson's rules converge *much faster* than Trapezoidal — a direct consequence of $O(h^4)$ vs $O(h^2)$ error.
